<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Computerized PMIS. List any four of its core functions.  *(3 Marks)*

**Model Answer:**

> A **Computerized Project Management Information System (C-PMIS)** is an integrated, IT-enabled framework that uses software, databases, and network infrastructure to collect, store, process, and disseminate project information across the project life cycle, enabling real-time decision-making and stakeholder communication.

**Four core functions:**
1. **Scope Management** — WBS creation and scope-change control.
2. **Schedule Management** — CPM, Gantt chart, baseline tracking.
3. **Cost Management** — Budgeting, EVM, forecasting.
4. **Communication Management** — Automated reporting, stakeholder portals.

> **Examiner's Note (1 mark reserved):** The word **"automated"** or **"computerized"** must appear in the definition to secure full marks.

---

### Question 2: Differentiate between On-Premise and Cloud-Based PMIS.  *(3 Marks)*

| Aspect | On-Premise PMIS | Cloud-Based PMIS |
|---|---|---|
| **Location** | Installed on company servers | Hosted on vendor's cloud (AWS/Azure) |
| **Cost** | High upfront capex | Pay-as-you-go subscription (SaaS) |
| **Maintenance** | Internal IT team | Vendor-managed |
| **Accessibility** | Limited to office network | Anywhere with internet |
| **Security** | Full internal control | Vendor's SLA + encryption |
| **Scalability** | Limited by hardware | Elastic, near-infinite |

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks):** [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Style]

**(a)** Explain the **five-layer architecture** of a Computerized PMIS with a neat block diagram. Describe the role of each layer in detail. **\[7 Marks\]**

**(b)** A software development project has BAC = ₹ 80 Lakhs, PV = ₹ 40 Lakhs, EV = ₹ 35 Lakhs, AC = ₹ 45 Lakhs at the end of month 4.
Calculate CV, SV, CPI, SPI, EAC, VAC and TCPI. Interpret each value and suggest two corrective actions. **\[7 Marks\]**

---

#### Model Solution to Question A(a):

The **five-layer architecture** of a Computerized PMIS is explained using the **IDCRO model** (Input → Database → Computation → Reporting → Output):

1. **Layer 1 — Data Acquisition (Input):** Captures raw data via timesheets, IoT sensors, mobile apps, API integrations. Validates using rule engines.
2. **Layer 2 — Database / Repository:** Stores structured data in SQL DBs and unstructured data (documents) in vaults. Maintains audit trails and baselines.
3. **Layer 3 — Processing Engine:** Runs CPM, EVM, Monte Carlo, and ML-based forecasting algorithms.
4. **Layer 4 — Reporting:** Produces dashboards, Gantt charts, S-curves, executive summaries in PDF/Excel.
5. **Layer 5 — Communication / Distribution:** Pushes data via email, SMS, mobile apps, MS Teams, and stakeholder portals.

> **Block Diagram:** Already provided in SECTION 4, Figure 4.1. (Students must redraw it in the answer script.)

---

#### Model Solution to Question A(b):

**Given:** $BAC = 80L$, $PV = 40L$, $EV = 35L$, $AC = 45L$

**Step 1 — Cost Variance:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
CV &= EV - AC \\
&= 35 - 45 \\
&= -10 \; \text{Lakhs}
\end{aligned}
$$

⇒ Over budget by ₹ 10 Lakhs. ⚠

**Step 2 — Schedule Variance:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
SV &= EV - PV \\
&= 35 - 40 \\
&= -5 \; \text{Lakhs}
\end{aligned}
$$

⇒ Behind schedule. ⚠

**Step 3 — CPI:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
CPI &= \frac{EV}{AC} = \frac{35}{45} = 0.778
\end{aligned}
$$

⇒ Inefficient ($< 1$). ⚠

**Step 4 — SPI:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
SPI &= \frac{EV}{PV} = \frac{35}{40} = 0.875
\end{aligned}
$$

⇒ Slow progress. ⚠

**Step 5 — EAC:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
EAC &= \frac{BAC}{CPI} = \frac{80}{0.778} = ₹ \; 102.83 \; \text{Lakhs}
\end{aligned}
$$

⇒ Forecasted final cost overrun of ₹ 22.83 Lakhs.

**Step 6 — VAC:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
VAC &= BAC - EAC = 80 - 102.83 = -22.83 \; \text{Lakhs}
\end{aligned}
$$

⇒ Budget deficit at completion. ⚠

**Step 7 — TCPI:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
TCPI &= \frac{BAC - EV}{BAC - AC} = \frac{80 - 35}{80 - 45} = \frac{45}{35} = 1.286
\end{aligned}
$$

⇒ Remaining work must be done at 128.6% efficiency to meet BAC — unrealistic. **Recommend re-baselining.**

**Two Corrective Actions:**
1. **Crashing the critical path:** Add overtime or extra developers to recover schedule ($SPI < 1$).
2. **Re-baselining BAC:** Submit formal change request to revise BAC from ₹ 80L to ₹ 102.83L with sponsor approval.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks):** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Style] *(Alternative Choice)*

**(a)** Discuss the **advantages and limitations** of a Computerized PMIS. Compare any three popular PMIS tools (e.g., MS Project, Primavera P6, Jira). **\[7 Marks\]**

**(b)** What is **Earned Value Management (EVM)**? Explain any four EVM indices with their formulas and interpretation rules. **\[7 Marks\]**

---

#### Model Solution to Question B(a):

**Advantages of Computerized PMIS:**
- Centralized data; eliminates conflicting spreadsheets.
- Real-time dashboards; faster decision-making.
- Automated calculations of CPM and EVM.
- Improved stakeholder communication.
- Audit trail for compliance.

**Limitations:**
- High initial cost and training overhead.
- Resistance to adoption from team.
- Data quality dependency.
- Vendor lock-in and migration challenges.

**Comparison of Three PMIS Tools:**

| Feature | MS Project | Primavera P6 | Jira |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Best For** | Small-medium projects | Large engineering/construction | Agile software projects |
| **Scheduling** | Gantt, CPM | Advanced CPM, resource leveling | Scrum/Kanban boards |
| **Cost Mgmt** | Basic | Advanced | Limited |
| **User Base** | Wide (Microsoft ecosystem) | Construction/EPC majors | Software teams |
| **Pricing** | Moderate | High | Free tier + paid |
| **Cloud** | Yes (Project Online) | Yes | Yes (Atlassian Cloud) |

---

#### Model Solution to Question B(b):

**EVM Definition:** Earned Value Management is a **performance measurement technique** that integrates scope, schedule, and cost to assess project progress objectively. It compares *planned work*, *actual work done*, and *actual cost spent* to compute variances and forecasts.

**Four EVM Indices:**

| Index | Formula | Interpretation |
|---|---|---|
| **CV** | $EV - AC$ | $< 0$ = over budget |
| **SV** | $EV - PV$ | $< 0$ = behind schedule |
| **CPI** | $EV / AC$ | $< 1$ = cost inefficient |
| **SPI** | $EV / PV$ | $< 1$ = schedule delay |

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. ❌ Writing only **abbreviations** (CV, SV) without **full forms** → lose 1 mark.
> 2. ❌ Not writing the **unit** (Rupees, Lakhs, Crores) → lose 0.5 mark per formula.
> 3. ❌ Skipping the **interpretation** step after each calculation → lose 1–2 marks.
> 4. ❌ Using vertical pipes `|` inside markdown tables (will break rendering) → in exam script, write clear sentences instead.
> 5. ❌ Confusing **EAC** with **ETC** — remember ETC = remaining cost, EAC = total final cost.
> 6. ❌ Forgetting to draw the **block diagram** for "Explain PMIS architecture" — examiners allocate 1 mark specifically for the diagram.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap — High-Density Revision Checklist

> [!TIP]
> **Read this list 5 minutes before entering the exam hall.**

- ✅ **PMIS = People + Process + Tools + Information** (memorize the 4 pillars).
- ✅ **Computerized PMIS** = software-driven, automated, real-time, integrated.
- ✅ **Architecture (IDCRO):** Input → Database → Computation → Reporting → Output.
- ✅ **Functions (8 modules):** Scope, Schedule, Cost, Resource, Risk, Quality, Communication, Procurement.
- ✅ **EVM Core Formulas:** CV, SV, CPI, SPI, EAC, ETC, VAC, TCPI — know all 8 by heart.
- ✅ **CPI < 1 ⇒ Over budget; SPI < 1 ⇒ Behind schedule.**
- ✅ **EAC = BAC / CPI** (when performance is uniform).
- ✅ **TCPI > 1 ⇒ Need higher future efficiency to meet BAC.**
- ✅ **Tools:** MS Project (small), Primavera P6 (large EPC), Jira (Agile).
- ✅ **Advantages:** centralization, automation, real-time data, audit trail, scalability.
- ✅ **Limitations:** cost, learning curve, data quality, vendor lock-in.
- ✅ **Always draw block diagram** in architecture questions (1 reserved mark).
- ✅ **Always write full form** before abbreviation (1 reserved mark).
- ✅ **Cloud-based PMIS** = SaaS, lower upfront cost, accessible anywhere.
- ✅ **On-premise PMIS** = higher control, higher capex, internal IT dependency.
- ✅ **Sample numerical problem** is the most repeated 14-mark pattern — practice it twice.
- ✅ Mention **PMBOK® 7th Edition** and **ISO 21500** in theory answers to sound academically rigorous.

> **Best of luck with your KTU Module 3 examination — you are now exam-ready!**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Earned Value Analysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT863 - PROJECT MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Earned Value Analysis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Earned Value Analysis (EVA)** is a quantitative project performance measurement technique that integrates **scope, schedule, and cost** dimensions of a project into a unified, single-value management framework. It compares the *amount of work actually completed* against the *amount of work planned to be completed* and the *actual cost incurred* for that completed work.

Formally, EVA is a three-point comparison system that answers three fundamental project questions simultaneously:
- **What did we plan to do?** → Captured by *Planned Value (PV)*
- **What did we actually do?** → Captured by *Earned Value (EV)*
- **What did it cost us to do it?** → Captured by *Actual Cost (AC)*

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Earned Value Analysis is a **mandatory analytical tool** under the "Project Monitoring & Control" section of Module 3. It is one of the most frequently tested topics in KTU ESE examinations and carries high weightage in numerical problems.

The technique is officially codified in the **PMBOK Guide (Project Management Body of Knowledge)** under the *Monitor and Control Project Work* knowledge area and falls under the **Cost Management** discipline.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The Road-Trip Analogy — Making EVA Instantly Clear**

Imagine you are driving from **Kochi to Delhi** (≈ 2,500 km) with a budget of **₹20,000** for fuel, and you plan to reach halfway (**1,250 km**) by **Day 2**.

- **Planned Value (PV)** = "On Day 2, my GPS says I *should* be in Bhopal (1,250 km mark)."
- **Earned Value (EV)** = "On Day 2, I look at my odometer and find I am actually only at **Nagpur** (1,000 km mark)."
- **Actual Cost (AC)** = "My fuel bills and toll receipts so far total **₹11,000**."

From this, we instantly see:
- I am **behind schedule** (EV < PV by 250 km).
- I am **over budget** (costing more per km than expected).

EVA replicates this exact logic for projects. It is the **odometer + fuel gauge + GPS** of project management.

---

## 1.3 The Three Foundational Parameters

| Parameter | Also Known As | Meaning | Question Answered |
|-----------|---------------|---------|-------------------|
| **PV (Planned Value)** | BCWS — Budgeted Cost of Work Scheduled | The authorized budget assigned to scheduled work | *What did we plan to accomplish by now?* |
| **EV (Earned Value)** | BCWP — Budgeted Cost of Work Performed | The budgeted cost for work actually completed | *What did we actually accomplish by now?* |
| **AC (Actual Cost)** | ACWP — Actual Cost of Work Performed | The real cost incurred for the work done | *What did it cost us to accomplish this?* |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Distinction:** EV is **not** the actual money earned by the project. It is the *value of work completed*, *measured in budgeted terms*, not in rupees actually received. This is the single most common conceptual error students make in KTU exams.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundations

EVA is built upon the **Performance Measurement Baseline (PMB)** — a time-phased budget plan that establishes what work is scheduled and how much budget is allotted to it across the project lifecycle.

The analysis proceeds in three logical stages:

### Stage A — Establish the Baseline
The project is decomposed (typically via a **Work Breakdown Structure**) and each activity is assigned:
- A **budget** (in monetary terms)
- A **duration** (in time units)
- A **schedule** (when it is planned to occur)

This produces the **PV curve** — a cumulative S-curve showing planned cumulative cost over time.

### Stage B — Track Actual Performance
At any reporting point (status date / data date):
- **AC** is collected from accounting/finance records.
- **EV** is calculated based on **percent complete** of each work package.
$$ EV = \text{Percent Complete} \times \text{Activity Budget} $$

### Stage C — Analyze Variances & Forecast
Variances flag deviations; indices measure efficiency; forecasts extrapolate final project outcomes.

---

## 2.2 Variance Analysis (Absolute Measures)

Variances are **absolute deviations** measured in currency units (₹, $, etc.). They indicate *how much* the project has deviated, not the *rate* of deviation.

| Variance | Formula | Interpretation |
|----------|---------|----------------|
| **Schedule Variance (SV)** | $SV = EV - PV$ | $SV > 0$: Ahead of schedule; $SV < 0$: Behind schedule |
| **Cost Variance (CV)** | $CV = EV - AC$ | $CV > 0$: Under budget; $CV < 0$: Over budget |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pitfall Alert:** A negative SV does **NOT** mean the project is delayed in calendar time — it means the project is *behind in earned value terms*. A project can be on calendar schedule but have negative SV if less work was completed than planned.

---

## 2.3 Performance Indices (Relative / Efficiency Measures)

Indices are **ratios (dimensionless numbers)** that measure the *rate* of efficiency. They are superior to variances because they remain valid regardless of project size.

| Index | Formula | Interpretation |
|-------|---------|----------------|
| **Schedule Performance Index (SPI)** | $SPI = \dfrac{EV}{PV}$ | $SPI > 1$: Ahead of schedule; $SPI < 1$: Behind schedule |
| **Cost Performance Index (CPI)** | $CPI = \dfrac{EV}{AC}$ | $CPI > 1$: Under budget (efficient); $CPI < 1$: Over budget (inefficient) |

---

## 2.4 Forecasting Models (Predicting Final Outcomes)

Using current performance to predict where the project is heading:

| Forecast | Formula | Meaning |
|----------|---------|---------|
| **EAC (Estimate at Completion)** | $EAC = \dfrac{BAC}{CPI}$ | Total expected cost of the project at completion |
| **ETC (Estimate to Complete)** | $ETC = EAC - AC$ | Remaining funds needed to finish the project |
| **VAC (Variance at Completion)** | $VAC = BAC - EAC$ | Expected budget surplus (+) or deficit (−) at project end |
| **TCPI (To-Complete Performance Index)** | $TCPI = \dfrac{BAC - EV}{BAC - AC}$ | Cost-efficiency required *from now on* to meet BAC |

Where **BAC (Budget at Completion)** = Total approved project budget.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Alternative EAC Formulas (commonly tested in KTU):**
> 1. $EAC = AC + ETC$ (generic)
> 2. $EAC = AC + \dfrac{BAC - EV}{CPI}$ (using current cost efficiency for remaining work)
> 3. $EAC = AC + (BAC - EV)$ (assuming remaining work will be done at planned budget)
> 4. $EAC = \dfrac{BAC}{CPI}$ (assuming current CPI persists — most commonly used in KTU)

---

## 2.5 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Complete EVA Formula Reference (Memorize This Table)**

| # | Metric | Formula | Unit | Healthy Value |
|---|--------|---------|------|---------------|
| 1 | Planned Value | $PV$ | ₹ | Baseline |
| 2 | Earned Value | $EV = \% \text{ Complete} \times \text{Budget}$ | ₹ | — |
| 3 | Actual Cost | $AC$ | ₹ | — |
| 4 | Budget at Completion | $BAC$ | ₹ | — |
| 5 | Cost Variance | $CV = EV - AC$ | ₹ | $\geq 0$ |
| 6 | Schedule Variance | $SV = EV - PV$ | ₹ | $\geq 0$ |
| 7 | Cost Performance Index | $CPI = \dfrac{EV}{AC}$ | ratio | $\geq 1$ |
| 8 | Schedule Performance Index | $SPI = \dfrac{EV}{PV}$ | ratio | $\geq 1$ |
| 9 | Estimate at Completion | $EAC = \dfrac{BAC}{CPI}$ | ₹ | $\leq BAC$ |
| 10 | Estimate to Complete | $ETC = EAC - AC$ | ₹ | — |
| 11 | Variance at Completion | $VAC = BAC - EAC$ | ₹ | $\geq 0$ |
| 12 | To-Complete Performance Index | $TCPI = \dfrac{BAC - EV}{BAC - AC}$ | ratio | — |

---

## 2.6 Real-World Utility in Engineering

EVA is indispensable in industries such as:
- **Aerospace & Defense** (ISRO, DRDO projects — multi-thousand crore budgets)
- **Civil Construction** (Kerala infrastructure projects — metro rail, bridges)
- **IT & Software** (Agile sprints use a simplified EVA-style burndown chart)
- **Manufacturing** (production line cost-time integration)

It enables early **diagnostic warnings** (typically when SPI or CPI drops below **0.9**), allowing managers to initiate **corrective actions** before crises occur.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Comprehensive Numerical Problem (Full Worked Solution)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Statement (KTU-Style):**
> A KTU-affiliated engineering college is implementing an **ERP system deployment** project. The total approved budget (**BAC**) is **₹10,00,000** spread over **10 months**.
>
> At the end of **Month 5**, the project manager reports the following status:
> - **Planned completion** by Month 5 = **50%** of total work
> - **Actual work completed** by Month 5 = **40%** of total work
> - **Actual cost incurred** by Month 5 = **₹5,50,000**
>
> Compute: PV, EV, SV, CV, SPI, CPI, EAC, ETC, VAC, and TCPI. Provide a comprehensive interpretation.

---

### Step 1 — Compute the Three Baseline Parameters

**Planned Value (PV):**
The budget for the work that *should* have been done by Month 5 is 50% of BAC.

$$
\begin{aligned}
PV &= 50\% \times BAC \\
   &= 0.50 \times 10{,}00{,}000 \\
   &= \text{₹ } 5{,}00{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Earned Value (EV):**
The budget for the work *actually* completed (40% of total).

$$
\begin{aligned}
EV &= 40\% \times BAC \\
   &= 0.40 \times 10{,}00{,}000 \\
   &= \text{₹ } 4{,}00{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Actual Cost (AC):**
Directly given — the actual money spent.

$$
AC = \text{₹ } 5{,}50{,}000
$$

---

### Step 2 — Compute Variances

**Schedule Variance (SV):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
SV &= EV - PV \\
   &= 4{,}00{,}000 - 5{,}00{,}000 \\
   &= -\text{₹ } 1{,}00{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

*Negative SV → Project is behind schedule in value terms.*

**Cost Variance (CV):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
CV &= EV - AC \\
   &= 4{,}00{,}000 - 5{,}50{,}000 \\
   &= -\text{₹ } 1{,}50{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

*Negative CV → Project is over budget.*

---

### Step 3 — Compute Performance Indices

**Schedule Performance Index (SPI):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
SPI &= \dfrac{EV}{PV} \\
    &= \dfrac{4{,}00{,}000}{5{,}00{,}000} \\
    &= 0.80
\end{aligned}
$$

*SPI < 1 → For every ₹1 of planned work, only ₹0.80 worth was actually completed.*

**Cost Performance Index (CPI):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
CPI &= \dfrac{EV}{AC} \\
    &= \dfrac{4{,}00{,}000}{5{,}50{,}000} \\
    &= 0.7273 \text{ (rounded to 4 decimals)}
\end{aligned}
$$

*CPI < 1 → For every ₹1 spent, only ₹0.7273 worth of work was accomplished.*

---

### Step 4 — Forecast Final Project Outcomes

**Estimate at Completion (EAC):**
Assuming current cost efficiency persists.

$$
\begin{aligned}
EAC &= \dfrac{BAC}{CPI} \\
    &= \dfrac{10{,}00{,}000}{0.7273} \\
    &= \text{₹ } 13{,}74{,}941.78
\end{aligned}
$$

*The project is now expected to cost ₹13.75 lakh instead of the planned ₹10 lakh.*

**Estimate to Complete (ETC):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
ETC &= EAC - AC \\
    &= 13{,}74{,}941.78 - 5{,}50{,}000 \\
    &= \text{₹ } 8{,}24{,}941.78
\end{aligned}
$$

*Remaining work will require approximately ₹8.25 lakh.*

**Variance at Completion (VAC):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
VAC &= BAC - EAC \\
    &= 10{,}00{,}000 - 13{,}74{,}941.78 \\
    &= -\text{₹ } 3{,}74{,}941.78
\end{aligned}
$$

*Negative VAC → Expected overrun of approximately ₹3.75 lakh at project completion.*

**To-Complete Performance Index (TCPI):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
TCPI &= \dfrac{BAC - EV}{BAC - AC} \\
     &= \dfrac{10{,}00{,}000 - 4{,}00{,}000}{10{,}00{,}000 - 5{,}50{,}000} \\
     &= \dfrac{6{,}00{,}000}{4{,}50{,}000} \\
     &= 1.3333
\end{aligned}
$$

*TCPI > 1 → The remaining work must be performed at 133.33% cost-efficiency to still meet the original BAC — an extremely aggressive target.*

---

## 3.2 Interpretation Matrix (Consolidated Status Report)

| Metric | Computed Value | Status | Health |
|--------|---------------|--------|--------|
| PV | ₹ 5,00,000 | Baseline | — |
| EV | ₹ 4,00,000 | — | — |
| AC | ₹ 5,50,000 | — | — |
| SV | −₹ 1,00,000 | Behind schedule | ⚠️ Warning |
| CV | −₹ 1,50,000 | Over budget | ⚠️ Warning |
| SPI | 0.80 | Inefficient scheduling | 🔴 Critical |
| CPI | 0.7273 | Inefficient cost use | 🔴 Critical |
| EAC | ₹ 13,74,941.78 | Overrun expected | 🔴 Critical |
| ETC | ₹ 8,24,941.78 | High remaining cost | ⚠️ Warning |
| VAC | −₹ 3,74,941.78 | Deficit projected | 🔴 Critical |
| TCPI | 1.3333 | Very demanding target | 🔴 Critical |

**Final Verdict:** The project is in a **double-trouble scenario** — simultaneously behind schedule AND over budget. **Immediate corrective action** (scope reduction, resource augmentation, fast-tracking, or crashing) is recommended.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 EVA Process Flow Diagram (Mermaid)

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Project Initiation and Planning] --> B[Develop Work Breakdown Structure]
    B --> C[Create Performance Measurement Baseline]
    C --> D[Generate PV Curve Planned Value over Time]
    D --> E[Execute Project Activities]
    E --> F[Measure Percent Complete at Status Date]
    F --> G[Collect Actual Cost AC from Accounts]
    G --> H[Compute Earned Value EV = Percent Complete times Budget]
    H --> I[Calculate Schedule Variance SV and Cost Variance CV]
    G --> I
    D --> I
    I --> J[Calculate SPI equals EV divided by PV]
    I --> K[Calculate CPI equals EV divided by AC]
    J --> L[Forecast EAC equals BAC divided by CPI]
    K --> L
    L --> M{Is SPI and CPI Greater Than or Equal to 1?}
    M -->|Yes| N[Project on Track Continue Monitoring]
    M -->|No| O[Trigger Corrective Actions]
    O --> P[Replan Adjust Resources or Scope]
    P --> E
    N --> Q[Periodic Status Reporting to Stakeholders]
```

## 4.2 EVA Input-Output Block Diagram (Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
graph LR
    INPUT1[Cost Baseline BAC] --> ENGINE[EVA Computation Engine]
    INPUT2[Schedule Plan] --> ENGINE
    INPUT3[Work Packages] --> ENGINE
    INPUT4[Percent Complete Data] --> ENGINE
    INPUT5[Actual Cost Invoices] --> ENGINE
    ENGINE --> OUT1[Variances SV and CV]
    ENGINE --> OUT2[Indices SPI and CPI]
    ENGINE --> OUT3[Forecasts EAC ETC VAC]
    ENGINE --> OUT4[TCPI Performance Target]
    OUT1 --> DECISION[Management Decision Support System]
    OUT2 --> DECISION
    OUT3 --> DECISION
    OUT4 --> DECISION
    DECISION --> ACT1[Continue as Planned]
    DECISION --> ACT2[Take Corrective Action]
    DECISION --> ACT3[Reforecast and Replan]
    DECISION --> ACT4[Escalate to Senior Management]
```

## 4.3 Decision Matrix Based on CPI and SPI

```mermaid
graph TD
    START[Evaluate CPI and SPI at Status Date] --> Q1{Is CPI Less Than 1?}
    Q1 -->|Yes| Q2{Is SPI Less Than 1?}
    Q1 -->|No| Q3{Is SPI Less Than 1?}
    Q2 -->|Yes| R1[Critical: Cost Overrun AND Schedule Slippage - Immediate Action Required]
    Q2 -->|No| R2[Cost Overrun but on Schedule - Investigate Cost Causes]
    Q3 -->|Yes| R3[Schedule Slippage but Under Budget - Investigate Schedule Causes]
    Q3 -->|No| R4[Healthy State - Project on Track - Continue Monitoring]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading the Decision Matrix:**
> - **Bottom-right cell (CPI ≥ 1, SPI ≥ 1):** Healthy — all green.
> - **Top-left cell (CPI < 1, SPI < 1):** Critical — both indices red. This is the worst-case scenario and demands immediate management intervention.
> - **Off-diagonal cells:** Asymmetric issues — investigate the specific dimension causing the problem.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

---

## PART A QUESTIONS (3 MARKS EACH)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam — May 2023, Model Paper Set B]`
**Define Earned Value Analysis. List any THREE performance metrics used in EVA with their significance.**

**Model Answer:**

Earned Value Analysis (EVA) is a project performance measurement technique that integrates scope, schedule, and cost to assess project progress. It compares the planned work (PV), work actually performed (EV), and the actual cost incurred (AC) to determine variances and forecast outcomes.

**Three Performance Metrics:**

1. **Cost Variance (CV) = EV − AC** — Measures whether the project is under or over budget. Positive CV indicates cost efficiency; negative CV indicates cost overrun.

2. **Schedule Variance (SV) = EV − PV** — Measures whether the project is ahead or behind schedule in value terms. Positive SV indicates schedule advancement; negative SV indicates schedule slippage.

3. **Cost Performance Index (CPI) = EV/AC** — A ratio measuring cost efficiency. CPI ≥ 1 means every rupee spent is generating at least one rupee of value.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]`
**Distinguish between Earned Value (EV) and Planned Value (PV) with a suitable example.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Planned Value (PV) | Earned Value (EV) |
|--------|-------------------|-------------------|
| **Definition** | Budgeted cost of work *scheduled* to be completed by a given date | Budgeted cost of work *actually completed* by a given date |
| **Also Known As** | BCWS | BCWP |
| **Based On** | Project schedule (plan) | Actual physical progress (% complete) |
| **Nature** | Forward-looking | Backward-looking |

**Example:** Consider a project with BAC = ₹ 5,00,000 scheduled to be 60% complete by Day 30. By Day 30, only 50% work is actually done and ₹ 3,00,000 has been spent.
- PV = 60% × 5,00,000 = **₹ 3,00,000**
- EV = 50% × 5,00,000 = **₹ 2,50,000**
- AC = **₹ 3,00,000**

---

## PART B QUESTIONS (14 MARKS EACH — Internal Choice)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam — June 2024]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the THREE fundamental parameters of Earned Value Analysis with proper formulas. Discuss how Schedule Variance (SV) and Cost Variance (CV) are computed and interpreted.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A construction project has a Budget at Completion (BAC) of **₹ 20,00,000** scheduled over **12 months**. At the end of **Month 6**, the following data is reported:
- Planned % completion by Month 6 = **50%**
- Actual % completion by Month 6 = **45%**
- Actual Cost incurred = **₹ 11,00,000**

Compute PV, EV, AC, SV, CV, SPI, CPI, and EAC. Comment on the project health.

---

### Model Answer for Question A

#### Part (a) — Solution [7 Marks]

The **three fundamental parameters** of Earned Value Analysis are:

**(i) Planned Value (PV) — Also called Budgeted Cost of Work Scheduled (BCWS):**

$$ PV = \text{Total Budget} \times \text{Planned \% Complete at Status Date} $$

PV represents the *authorized budget* assigned to work that was *scheduled* to be completed by a specific point in time. It is established during project planning and forms the **baseline** against which performance is measured.

**(ii) Earned Value (EV) — Also called Budgeted Cost of Work Performed (BCWP):**

$$ EV = \text{Total Budget} \times \text{Actual \% Complete at Status Date} $$

EV is the *budgeted value* of work *actually accomplished* as measured by physical percent complete. It bridges the gap between what was planned (PV) and what was actually done.

**(iii) Actual Cost (AC) — Also called Actual Cost of Work Performed (ACWP):**

$$ AC = \text{Actual money spent on completed work at the status date} $$

AC is the *real cost incurred* for the work performed, drawn from accounting records. It is independent of planned or earned values.

**Computation and Interpretation of Variances:**

**Schedule Variance (SV):**
$$ SV = EV - PV $$

- $SV > 0$ → Project is **ahead of schedule** (more value earned than planned).
- $SV < 0$ → Project is **behind schedule** (less value earned than planned).
- $SV = 0$ → Project is **on schedule**.

**Cost Variance (CV):**
$$ CV = EV - AC $$

- $CV > 0$ → Project is **under budget** (less spent than the value of work done).
- $CV < 0$ → Project is **over budget** (more spent than the value of work done).
- $CV = 0$ → Project is **on budget**.

Variances are absolute deviations measured in **currency units**. They indicate *magnitude of deviation* but not *rate of efficiency*.

#### Part (b) — Solution [7 Marks]

**Given:**
- BAC = ₹ 20,00,000
- Planned % Complete at Month 6 = 50%
- Actual % Complete at Month 6 = 45%
- AC = ₹ 11,00,000

**Step 1 — Compute PV:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
PV &= 50\% \times 20{,}00{,}000 \\
   &= \text{₹ } 10{,}00{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute EV:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
EV &= 45\% \times 20{,}00{,}000 \\
   &= \text{₹ } 9{,}00{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — AC is given:**

$$ AC = \text{₹ } 11{,}00{,}000 $$

**Step 4 — Schedule Variance:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
SV &= EV - PV \\
   &= 9{,}00{,}000 - 10{,}00{,}000 \\
   &= -\text{₹ } 1{,}00{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

*Negative SV → Behind schedule.*

**Step 5 — Cost Variance:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
CV &= EV - AC \\
   &= 9{,}00{,}000 - 11{,}00{,}000 \\
   &= -\text{₹ } 2{,}00{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

*Negative CV → Over budget.*

**Step 6 — Schedule Performance Index:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
SPI &= \dfrac{EV}{PV} \\
    &= \dfrac{9{,}00{,}000}{10{,}00{,}000} \\
    &= 0.90
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7 — Cost Performance Index:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
CPI &= \dfrac{EV}{AC} \\
    &= \dfrac{9{,}00{,}000}{11{,}00{,}000} \\
    &= 0.8182
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 8 — Estimate at Completion:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
EAC &= \dfrac{BAC}{CPI} \\
    &= \dfrac{20{,}00{,}000}{0.8182} \\
    &= \text{₹ } 24{,}44{,}444
\end{aligned}
$$

**Comment on Project Health:**

| Metric | Value | Status |
|--------|-------|--------|
| SV | −₹ 1,00,000 | Behind schedule |
| CV | −₹ 2,00,000 | Over budget |
| SPI | 0.90 | Sub-optimal scheduling |
| CPI | 0.8182 | Poor cost efficiency |
| EAC | ₹ 24,44,444 | ₹ 4.44 lakh overrun expected |

**Interpretation:** The project is in a **double-critical state**. Both cost and schedule performances are below the acceptable threshold (SPI and CPI both below 1). The project is forecasted to overrun the budget by approximately ₹ 4.44 lakh. **Corrective actions such as crashing, fast-tracking, scope reduction, or resource reallocation must be initiated immediately.**

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]` *(ALTERNATIVE CHOICE)*

**(a) [7 Marks]** Define the following terms with formulas: (i) Cost Performance Index (CPI), (ii) Schedule Performance Index (SPI), (iii) To-Complete Performance Index (TCPI). Explain the significance of each.

**(b) [7 Marks]** An IT software project has BAC = **₹ 50,00,000**. After 8 months of a 12-month project, the status report shows:
- EV = **₹ 20,00,000**
- PV = **₹ 25,00,000**
- AC = **₹ 22,00,000**

Calculate: SV, CV, SPI, CPI, EAC, ETC, VAC, and TCPI. Interpret the findings.

---

### Model Answer for Question B

#### Part (a) — Solution [7 Marks]

**(i) Cost Performance Index (CPI):**
$$ CPI = \dfrac{EV}{AC} $$
It is a **dimensionless ratio** representing the cost efficiency of work performed. CPI ≥ 1 indicates cost-efficient execution (every rupee spent is producing at least one rupee of earned value). CPI < 1 signals cost overrun. CPI is the **most-watched metric** in EVA because it directly quantifies the financial health of the project.

**(ii) Schedule Performance Index (SPI):**
$$ SPI = \dfrac{EV}{PV} $$
It measures schedule efficiency — the ratio of earned value to planned value. SPI ≥ 1 means the project is progressing at or faster than planned. SPI < 1 indicates schedule slippage. SPI = 1.0 means the project is exactly on schedule.

**(iii) To-Complete Performance Index (TCPI):**
$$ TCPI = \dfrac{BAC - EV}{BAC - AC} $$
TCPI represents the **cost-efficiency level that the remaining work must achieve** in order to bring the project in at the original BAC. A TCPI greater than 1 indicates that future performance must be **more efficient than past performance** to meet the budget — often a warning sign that the BAC target may be unattainable.

#### Part (b) — Solution [7 Marks]

**Given:**
- BAC = ₹ 50,00,000
- EV = ₹ 20,00,000
- PV = ₹ 25,00,000
- AC = ₹ 22,00,000

**Step 1 — Schedule Variance:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
SV &= EV - PV \\
   &= 20{,}00{,}000 - 25{,}00{,}000 \\
   &= -\text{₹ } 5{,}00{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Cost Variance:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
CV &= EV - AC \\
   &= 20{,}00{,}000 - 22{,}00{,}000 \\
   &= -\text{₹ } 2{,}00{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Schedule Performance Index:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
SPI &= \dfrac{EV}{PV} \\
    &= \dfrac{20{,}00{,}000}{25{,}00{,}000} \\
    &= 0.80
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Cost Performance Index:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
CPI &= \dfrac{EV}{AC} \\
    &= \dfrac{20{,}00{,}000}{22{,}00{,}000} \\
    &= 0.9091
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Estimate at Completion:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
EAC &= \dfrac{BAC}{CPI} \\
    &= \dfrac{50{,}00{,}000}{0.9091} \\
    &= \text{₹ } 55{,}00{,}055
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Estimate to Complete:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
ETC &= EAC - AC \\
    &= 55{,}00{,}055 - 22{,}00{,}000 \\
    &= \text{₹ } 33{,}00{,}055
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7 — Variance at Completion:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
VAC &= BAC - EAC \\
    &= 50{,}00{,}000 - 55{,}00{,}055 \\
    &= -\text{₹ } 5{,}00{,}055
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 8 — To-Complete Performance Index:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
TCPI &= \dfrac{BAC - EV}{BAC - AC} \\
     &= \dfrac{50{,}00{,}000 - 20{,}00{,}000}{50{,}00{,}000 - 22{,}00{,}000} \\
     &= \dfrac{30{,}00{,}000}{28{,}00{,}000} \\
     &= 1.0714
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:**

| Metric | Value | Interpretation |
|--------|-------|----------------|
| SV | −₹ 5,00,000 | Behind schedule by ₹ 5 lakh in value terms |
| CV | −₹ 2,00,000 | Over budget by ₹ 2 lakh |
| SPI | 0.80 | For every ₹ 1 of planned work, only ₹ 0.80 was earned |
| CPI | 0.9091 | For every ₹ 1 spent, ₹ 0.9091 of value was earned |
| EAC | ₹ 55,00,055 | Expected total cost will exceed BAC by ~₹ 5 lakh |
| ETC | ₹ 33,00,055 | Remaining work needs ~₹ 33 lakh |
| VAC | −₹ 5,00,055 | Negative — budget deficit expected |
| TCPI | 1.0714 | Future work must be 7.14% more efficient than current pace |

**Overall Verdict:** The project is **behind schedule and over budget**, though the cost performance (CPI = 0.91) is better than the schedule performance (SPI = 0.80). A **TCPI of 1.07** is achievable with modest improvements in cost discipline. Management should prioritize **schedule recovery** through fast-tracking while maintaining current cost-efficiency levels.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing EV with revenue or profit** — EV is *budgeted cost* of work done, NOT money received. This conceptual error costs 1–2 marks per question.
> 2. **Using wrong base for EAC formula** — Many students write $EAC = BAC \times CPI$ instead of $EAC = BAC / CPI$. This is a *critical* formula error.
> 3. **Interpreting SV as calendar time variance** — SV is measured in cost units (₹), not in days. Always state SV in monetary terms.
> 4. **Forgetting to specify units** — KTU valuators deduct 0.5 marks for missing ₹ symbol or percentage notation.
> 5. **Computing TCPI incorrectly** — The denominator is $(BAC - AC)$, NOT $(EAC - AC)$ or $(BAC - EV)$.
> 6. **Not interpreting the result** — A numerical answer without a qualitative comment loses 2 marks in KTU ESE. Always end with an interpretation.

---

## 📌 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist for Earned Value Analysis**

- ✅ EVA integrates **scope, schedule, and cost** into a unified measurement system.
- ✅ The **three foundational parameters** are **PV** (plan), **EV** (progress), **AC** (spending).
- ✅ **Variances** (SV, CV) are *absolute deviations in ₹*. **Indices** (SPI, CPI) are *ratios* — preferred because they are project-size-independent.
- ✅ **SPI < 1** → Behind schedule; **CPI < 1** → Over budget; both are red flags when below **0.9**.
- ✅ **EAC** predicts the final total cost; **ETC** predicts the cost of remaining work; **VAC** predicts the final budget variance.
- ✅ The most-tested EAC formula in KTU is $EAC = \dfrac{BAC}{CPI}$.
- ✅ **TCPI > 1** means future work must be *more efficient* than past work to meet BAC — a strong warning signal.
- ✅ **BAC − EV** = budget remaining for unfinished work; **BAC − AC** = funds remaining.
- ✅ EV is calculated as **% complete × total task budget**, NEVER as actual money earned.
- ✅ KTU typically tests EVA through **numerical problems worth 7–14 marks**, often combined with questions on cost control and forecasting.
- ✅ Always conclude your KTU answer with an **interpretation paragraph** stating project health and recommended actions.

> **End of Module 3 Notes — Earned Value Analysis** | *Prepared per KTU 2024 Scheme Standards*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Planning-Monitoring-Controlling Cycle
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PESFT863 - PROJECT MANAGEMENT > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Planning-Monitoring-Controlling Cycle ## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal KTU 2024 Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Planning–Monitoring–Controlling (PMC) Cycle:** A structured, iterative, and continuous management framework that integrates the three core project management functions — **Planning** (defining objectives, scope, schedule, cost, and resources), **Monitoring** (systematic tracking of project performance against the baseline plan), and **Controlling** (comparing actual performance with planned performance and initiating corrective/preventive actions) — to ensure that a project is delivered on time, within budget, and to the stipulated quality standards. It forms the **closed-loop feedback system** that governs the entire project life cycle under the **PMBOK (Project Management Body of Knowledge)** framework. In the KTU 2024 Scheme, the PMC Cycle is treated as the **operational backbone** of project execution. It is not a one-time activity but a recurring loop that runs concurrently across all knowledge areas — Scope, Schedule, Cost, Quality, Risk, Procurement, Communication, and Stakeholder Management. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Captain of a Ship" > [!NOTE] > **Plain-English Analogy:** Imagine you are the **captain of a large cargo ship** sailing from Kochi to Rotterdam. | Captain's Action | PMC Equivalent | Real-World Project Parallel | |---|---|---| | Charts the route, fuel, crew duties, and ETA **before** sailing | **PLANNING** | WBS, Gantt Chart, Budget, Risk Plan, RACI Matrix | | Constantly checks GPS, compass, weather radar, and speed log **during** the voyage | **MONITORING** | Earned Value Analysis, Status Reports, KPIs, Dashboards | | Adjusts course, speed, and fuel burn when storms hit or currents drift the ship off track | **CONTROLLING** | Change Requests, Corrective Actions, Rebaselining, Crashing/Fast-Tracking | Just as a captain never sails and "forgets" to monitor, a project manager never plans and "ignores" execution — the **three activities are inseparable and continuous**. ### 1.3 Key Standard Metrics in the PMC Cycle The following metrics are universally accepted and **bold-marked** as they appear in KTU textbooks and PMBOK: - **BAC** = Budget at Completion (the total approved budget) - **PV** = Planned Value (the authorized budget for work scheduled) - **EV** = Earned Value (the authorized budget for work actually performed) - **AC** = Actual Cost (the real cost incurred for work performed) - **CV, SV, CPI, SPI** = Cost/Schedule Variance and Performance Indices - **EAC, ETC, VAC, TCPI** = Forecasting metrics > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Module 3 explicitly tests the student's ability to **compute PV, EV, AC, CV, SV, CPI, SPI, and EAC**, interpret their meaning, and recommend **corrective actions** based on index values. ## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Three Phases — Operational Breakdown #### Phase 1: PLANNING (The "What, When, Who, How, How Much" Phase) - **Why?** Without a baseline, there is no reference to measure progress against. - **How?** The PM develops a **Project Management Plan** containing subsidiary plans: 1. **Scope Management Plan** → defines deliverables via the **Work Breakdown Structure (WBS)**. 2. **Schedule Management Plan** → defines the timeline using **Gantt Charts, Milestones, CPM/PERT networks**. 3. **Cost Management Plan** → defines the **Cost Baseline (BAC)**. 4. **Quality Management Plan** → defines quality standards and acceptance criteria. 5. **Risk Management Plan** → defines risk responses, owners, and triggers. 6. **Resource & Communication Plans** → define the **RACI Matrix** and reporting cadence. - **Output:** The **Performance Measurement Baseline (PMB)** = Scope + Schedule + Cost baselines. This becomes the **ruler** against which monitoring is conducted. #### Phase 2: MONITORING (The "What is Happening?" Phase) - **Why?** Plans are based on assumptions; reality always deviates. Monitoring captures deviations. - **How?** The PM collects **Work Performance Data** → analyzes it → produces **Work Performance Information**. Common Monitoring Tools & Techniques: - **Earned Value Management (EVM)** — the gold standard for integrated scope-schedule-cost monitoring. - **Status Reports & Dashboards** — weekly/monthly status to stakeholders. - **Risk Reassessments** — checking if identified risks have triggered. - **Variance Analysis** — comparing PV, EV, AC at every status date. - **Trend Analysis** — observing how CPI/SPI are moving over time. - **Change Log Review** — tracking pending and approved change requests. #### Phase 3: CONTROLLING (The "What Should We Do About It?" Phase) - **Why?** Monitoring without action is merely observation. Controlling converts data into decisions. - **How?** The PM takes **Work Performance Information** → recommends **Corrective / Preventive / Defect Repair Actions** → updates the **Project Management Plan** via the **Perform Integrated Change Control** process. Controlling Tools & Techniques: - **Corrective Actions** — bring future performance back in line with the plan (e.g., crashing, fast-tracking, scope reduction). - **Preventive Actions** — reduce the probability of a future negative event. - **Change Control System** — formal review and approval gate for any baseline modification. - **Rebaselining** — done only when scope/schedule changes are formally approved; it resets the PMB. - **Configuration Management** — controls physical and documentation artifacts. - **Performance Reviews** — periodic structured review meetings. > [!NOTE] > **The "Why" behind the Loop:** Monitoring and Controlling together form the **"Execute → Measure → Adjust"** feedback loop. The output of Controlling becomes the input of the next Planning iteration — this is what makes the system **closed-loop** and self-correcting. ### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Earned Value Management) > [!IMPORTANT] > **All formulas below are mathematically derivable from three base values: PV, EV, AC.** Memorize these in the exact order shown — KTU examiners follow this sequence in valuation keys. | # | Metric | Formula | Meaning | Decision Rule | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Cost Variance | $CV = EV - AC$ | Cost overrun (+) or saving (−) | $CV \geq 0$ = Under/On Budget | | 2 | Schedule Variance | $SV = EV - PV$ | Schedule ahead (+) or behind (−) | $SV \geq 0$ = Ahead/On Schedule | | 3 | Cost Performance Index | $CPI = \dfrac{EV}{AC}$ | Cost efficiency | $CPI \geq 1$ = Efficient | | 4 | Schedule Performance Index | $SPI = \dfrac{EV}{PV}$ | Schedule efficiency | $SPI \geq 1$ = Efficient | | 5 | Estimate at Completion (typical) | $EAC = \dfrac{BAC}{CPI}$ | Forecast final cost assuming current cost efficiency continues | Used when only cost variance is significant | | 6 | EAC (atypical, new plan) | $EAC = AC + (BAC - EV)$ | Forecast final cost assuming remaining work is done at original budget | Used when past performance is non-representative | | 7 | EAC (combined) | $EAC = AC + \dfrac{BAC - EV}{CPI \times SPI}$ | Forecast final cost assuming both cost and schedule efficiencies continue | Used when both indices matter | | 8 | Estimate to Complete | $ETC = EAC - AC$ | Cost needed to finish the project | Direct forecast | | 9 | Variance at Completion | $VAC = BAC - EAC$ | Budget surplus (+) or deficit (−) | $VAC \geq 0$ = Forecast on/below budget | | 10 | To-Complete Performance Index | $TCPI = \dfrac{BAC - EV}{BAC - AC}$ | Cost efficiency required from now on to finish on BAC | $TCPI \leq 1$ = Achievable | **Boundary Conditions Used by KTU Valuators:** - $EV \leq PV$ always (work performed cannot exceed work scheduled). - $EV \leq BAC$ (cannot earn more than the total budget). - If $CPI = SPI = 1$, then $EAC = BAC$ and $VAC = 0$. - All monetary values are in the **same currency unit (INR/USD)**; do not mix. ### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility In practice, the PMC cycle is the **daily operating rhythm** of construction projects, software rollouts, and product launches in Kerala industries (e.g., Infopark IT projects, Cochin Shipyard builds, Kochi Metro Phase-2 expansion, smart-city deployments). Without the PMC cycle, projects suffer the classic "**triple constraint failure**" — late delivery, cost overruns, and quality defects. EVM is mandated by the **U.S. DoD, NASA, and the Ministry of Statistics and Programme Implementation (MoSPI), Government of India** for all projects above ₹100 crore. ## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: From Base Values to Forecast (EAC) > [!IMPORTANT] > The relationship between the BAC, EV, AC, CPI, and EAC is **not arbitrary** — it is a direct consequence of the definition of "performance index." Below is the **exhaustive derivation** that KTU examiners expect a student to write when a question says *"Derive the formula for EAC."* **Given:** $$ CPI = \frac{EV}{AC} $$ **Assumption:** Past cost performance (CPI) is a reliable predictor of future cost performance. **Logical Statement:** If 1 unit of work has historically cost $\dfrac{AC}{EV}$ rupees, then the remaining work of value $(BAC - EV)$ will cost: $$ \text{Remaining Cost} = \left(\frac{AC}{EV}\right) \times (BAC - EV) $$ **Total Forecast Cost (EAC) = Past Actual Cost + Remaining Forecast Cost:** $$ EAC = AC + \left(\frac{AC}{EV}\right)(BAC - EV) $$ **Factor out AC:** $$ EAC = AC \left[1 + \frac{BAC - EV}{EV}\right] = AC \left[\frac{EV + BAC - EV}{EV}\right] = \frac{AC \times BAC}{EV} $$ **Substitute $AC = \dfrac{EV}{CPI}$:** $$ EAC = \frac{(EV / CPI) \times BAC}{EV} = \frac{BAC}{CPI} $$ **Hence Proved:** $$ \boxed{\,EAC = \dfrac{BAC}{CPI}\,} $$ --- ### 3.2 Derivation: To-Complete Performance Index (TCPI) **Goal:** Find the cost efficiency the project must achieve from *now* (status date) to *completion* so that the final cost equals BAC. **Remaining Budget Available:** $$ \text{Remaining Budget} = BAC - EV $$ **Remaining Cost to be Incurred:** $$ \text{Remaining Actual Cost} = BAC - AC $$ **TCPI Definition (cost efficiency needed for remaining work):** $$ TCPI = \frac{\text{Remaining Budget}}{\text{Remaining Cost to be Incurred}} $$ $$ TCPI = \frac{BAC - EV}{BAC - AC} $$ **Decision Rule (KTU valuation tip):** $$ TCPI \leq 1 \quad \text{(Achievable — can finish on BAC)} $$ $$ TCPI > 1 \quad \text{(Tough — must perform better than current pace)} $$ > [!NOTE] > If TCPI exceeds 1, the typical KTU answer concludes: *"A rebaselining of the BAC is required, or corrective actions (crashing/fast-tracking) must be initiated."* --- ### 3.3 Comprehensive Analytical Matrix (Interpretive Grid) This matrix is the **most exam-relevant table** for KTU answers where students are asked *"Interpret the project status."* | CPI | SPI | Project Status | Recommended Corrective Action | |---|---|---|---| | $> 1$ | $> 1$ | Excellent — under budget & ahead of schedule | Document best practices; consider scope addition | | $> 1$ | $< 1$ | Under budget but behind schedule | Fast-track or crash the schedule; resource augmentation | | $< 1$ | $> 1$ | Over budget but ahead of schedule | Investigate scope creep; enforce change control | | $< 1$ | $< 1$ | Critical — over budget and behind schedule | Full project review; rebaseline, scope reduction, or termination | | $= 1$ | $= 1$ | Perfect — exactly on plan | No action; continue monitoring | --- ### 3.4 Worked Numerical Walkthrough (KTU-Style Long Answer) **Problem:** A project has BAC = ₹10,00,000. At the status date (end of Month 4): - Planned Value (PV) = ₹4,00,000 - Earned Value (EV) = ₹3,50,000 - Actual Cost (AC) = ₹4,20,000 **Step 1: Cost Variance** $$ CV = EV - AC = 3{,}50{,}000 - 4{,}20{,}000 = -70{,}000 $$ **Interpretation:** Negative CV = project is **over budget by ₹70,000**. **Step 2: Schedule Variance** $$ SV = EV - PV = 3{,}50{,}000 - 4{,}00{,}000 = -50{,}000 $$ **Interpretation:** Negative SV = project is **behind schedule by ₹50,000 worth of work**. **Step 3: CPI** $$ CPI = \frac{EV}{AC} = \frac{3{,}50{,}000}{4{,}20{,}000} = 0.833 $$ **Interpretation:** For every ₹1 spent, only ₹0.83 of work is earned. **Step 4: SPI** $$ SPI = \frac{EV}{PV} = \frac{3{,}50{,}000}{4{,}00{,}000} = 0.875 $$ **Interpretation:** Only 87.5% of planned work is actually completed. **Step 5: EAC** $$ EAC = \frac{BAC}{CPI} = \frac{10{,}00{,}000}{0.833} = ₹12{,}00{,}480 $$ **Step 6: VAC** $$ VAC = BAC - EAC = 10{,}00{,}000 - 12{,}00{,}480 = -2{,}00{,}480 $$ **Conclusion:** Project will likely **overshoot its budget by ₹2,00,480** unless corrective actions (crashing, scope reduction, vendor renegotiation) are taken immediately. The project falls into the **"Critical" quadrant** (CPI < 1, SPI < 1) of the Analytical Matrix. ## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 The PMC Cycle — Closed-Loop Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TD startNode([Project Initiated]) --> planA[Planning Phase] planA --> planA1[Define Scope WBS] planA --> planA2[Build Schedule Gantt CPM] planA --> planA3[Estimate Cost BAC] planA --> planA4[Identify Risks] planA --> planA5[Assign Resources RACI] planA1 --> pmbNode[Performance Measurement Baseline PMB] planA2 --> pmbNode planA3 --> pmbNode planA4 --> pmbNode planA5 --> pmbNode pmbNode --> execNode[Execute Project Work] execNode --> monitorA[Monitoring Phase] monitorA --> monitorA1[Collect Work Performance Data] monitorA1 --> monitorA2[Compute PV EV AC] monitorA2 --> monitorA3[Calculate CV SV CPI SPI] monitorA3 --> monitorA4[Generate Status Reports] monitorA4 --> decisionNode{Deviation from Plan?} decisionNode -->|No Deviation| execNode decisionNode -->|Deviation Found| controlA[Controlling Phase] controlA --> controlA1[Variance Analysis] controlA1 --> controlA2[Recommend Corrective Action] controlA2 --> controlA3[Change Control Review] controlA3 --> controlA4[Update PMB if Approved] controlA4 --> planA controlA3 -->|Reject| execNode monitorA4 --> eacNode[Forecast EAC ETC VAC TCPI] eacNode --> decisionNode classDef planBox fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0d6efd,color:#000 classDef monitorBox fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#198754,color:#000 classDef controlBox fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#dc3545,color:#000 classDef decisionBox fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#fd7e14,color:#000 class planA,planA1,planA2,planA3,planA4,planA5,pmbNode planBox class monitorA,monitorA1,monitorA2,monitorA3,monitorA4,eacNode monitorBox class controlA,controlA1,controlA2,controlA3,controlA4 controlBox class decisionNode decisionBox ``` ### 4.2 Earned Value Status Quadrant (Mapping Project Health) ```mermaid flowchart LR q1[Excellent: CPI>1, SPI>1
Under Budget and Ahead] q2[Recovering: CPI1, SPI1
Over Budget and Behind] q3[Risky: CPI1, SPI1
Over Budget but Ahead] q4[Slow: CPI1, SPI1
Under Budget but Behind] center[(Project Status
At Reporting Date)] q1 --- center q2 --- center q3 --- center q4 --- center classDef greenBox fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#198754,color:#000 classDef redBox fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#dc3545,color:#000 classDef yellowBox fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#fd7e14,color:#000 classDef blueBox fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0d6efd,color:#000 class q1 greenBox class q2 redBox class q3 yellowBox class q4 blueBox ``` ### 4.3 Data Flow — From Plan to Decision ```mermaid flowchart LR planBaseline[Project Baseline
BAC PV Schedule] --> workExec[Work Execution] workExec --> workData[Work Performance Data] workData --> evmCalc[EVM Computation
EV AC CPI SPI] evmCalc --> wpi[Work Performance Information] wpi --> reportDash[Status Reports and Dashboards] wpi --> forecast[EAC ETC VAC TCPI] reportDash --> pmReview[PM Review Meeting] forecast --> pmReview pmReview --> changeReq{Change Needed?} changeReq -->|Yes| ccBoard[Change Control Board] changeReq -->|No| workExec ccBoard -->|Approved| planBaseline ccBoard -->|Rejected| workExec classDef baseBox fill:#e2e3e5,stroke:#6c757d,color:#000 classDef dataBox fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0d6efd,color:#000 classDef decBox fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#fd7e14,color:#000 class planBaseline,workExec baseBox class workData,evmCalc,wpi,reportDash,forecast dataBox class pmReview,changeReq,ccBoard decBox ``` > [!NOTE] > **Visualization Tip for KTU Exams:** When drawing the PMC cycle in an answer sheet, always use **circular arrows** to emphasize the **iterative, closed-loop nature** — examiners specifically reward this "feedback loop" depiction as it shows conceptual clarity. ## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`** **Q1. Define the Planning–Monitoring–Controlling cycle. Why is it considered a closed-loop system? (3 Marks)** **Model Answer:** The PMC cycle is a continuous project management framework consisting of three interdependent functions: **Planning** (developing the project baseline — scope, schedule, cost, quality, and risk plans), **Monitoring** (collecting and analyzing work performance data to compare against the baseline), and **Controlling** (initiating corrective, preventive, or change-control actions to bring performance back in line with the plan). It is a **closed-loop system** because the output of Controlling is fed back as updated input into the next iteration of Planning, creating a self-correcting feedback mechanism that runs throughout the project life cycle. The **Performance Measurement Baseline (PMB)** acts as the reference ruler for every loop iteration. --- > **`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`** **Q2. Differentiate between Monitoring and Controlling. (3 Marks)** **Model Answer:** | Aspect | Monitoring | Controlling | |---|---|---| | Purpose | To **observe and record** what is happening | To **act upon** the observations | | Nature | **Diagnostic** — measures deviations | **Therapeutic** — corrects deviations | | Output | Work Performance Information | Corrective/Preventive Actions & Change Requests | | Timing | Continuous throughout the project | Triggered when monitoring reveals a deviation | | Tools | EVM, KPIs, status reports, dashboards | Change Control System, crashing, fast-tracking, rebaselining | In short, **Monitoring tells you "the problem exists"**, while **Controlling decides "what to do about it."** --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > **`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper 2]`** **Question A (14 Marks)** **(a)** Explain in detail the three phases of the **Planning–Monitoring–Controlling cycle** with suitable examples from a construction project. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** A software project has a BAC of ₹20,00,000. At the end of Month 5, the data is: PV = ₹8,00,000, EV = ₹7,00,000, AC = ₹9,00,000. Compute CV, SV, CPI, SPI, EAC, ETC, VAC, and TCPI. Interpret the project status and recommend corrective actions. **(7 Marks)** #### Model Solution to Question A **Part (a) — Detailed Explanation with Construction Example:** **1. PLANNING (Pre-Construction & Mobilization Phase):** The PM of a Kochi Metro station project creates a WBS dividing the project into piers, deck, roofing, electrical, and finishing. A Gantt chart schedules each activity; a budget estimate of ₹150 crore is approved (BAC). A risk register identifies monsoon delays and labour shortages. A RACI matrix assigns responsibilities. **2. MONITORING (Execution Phase):** Each week, the site engineer submits a progress report. Earned Value calculations are done: if planned progress is 20% of pier work but only 14% is complete, the SPI is 0.70 — flagging a schedule slip. The cost team compares invoice totals with EV to compute CPI. **3. CONTROLLING (Steering Phase):** A CPI of 0.85 triggers a meeting where the PM decides to (i) crash critical path activities by adding a second shift, and (ii) renegotiate the concrete supplier rate. A change request is sent to the Change Control Board. If approved, the schedule is rebaselined. **Conclusion:** The three phases form a continuous loop — every week's monitoring may trigger a controlling action, which updates the plan for the next week. **Part (b) — Numerical Solution:** Given: BAC = ₹20,00,000, PV = ₹8,00,000, EV = ₹7,00,000, AC = ₹9,00,000. **Step 1: Cost Variance** $$ CV = EV - AC = 7{,}00{,}000 - 9{,}00{,}000 = -2{,}00{,}000 $$ The project is **over budget by ₹2,00,000**. **Step 2: Schedule Variance** $$ SV = EV - PV = 7{,}00{,}000 - 8{,}00{,}000 = -1{,}00{,}000 $$ The project is **behind schedule by ₹1,00,000 worth of work**. **Step 3: Cost Performance Index** $$ CPI = \frac{EV}{AC} = \frac{7{,}00{,}000}{9{,}00{,}000} = 0.778 $$ **Step 4: Schedule Performance Index** $$ SPI = \frac{EV}{PV} = \frac{7{,}00{,}000}{8{,}00{,}000} = 0.875 $$ **Step 5: Estimate at Completion** $$ EAC = \frac{BAC}{CPI} = \frac{20{,}00{,}000}{0.778} = ₹25{,}70{,}694 $$ **Step 6: Estimate to Complete** $$ ETC = EAC - AC = 25{,}70{,}694 - 9{,}00{,}000 = ₹16{,}70{,}694 $$ **Step 7: Variance at Completion** $$ VAC = BAC - EAC = 20{,}00{,}000 - 25{,}70{,}694 = -5{,}70{,}694 $$ **Step 8: To-Complete Performance Index** $$ TCPI = \frac{BAC - EV}{BAC - AC} = \frac{20{,}00{,}000 - 7{,}00{,}000}{20{,}00{,}000 - 9{,}00{,}000} = \frac{13{,}00{,}000}{11{,}00{,}000} = 1.182 $$ **Interpretation:** The project is in the **critical quadrant** (CPI < 1, SPI < 1) — over budget and behind schedule. The negative VAC of ₹5,70,694 signals a forecast budget overrun. **Recommended Corrective Actions:** 1. **Crashing** — add extra manpower/overtime to the critical path. 2. **Fast-tracking** — overlap sequential activities where possible. 3. **Scope reduction** — defer non-essential features. 4. **Vendor renegotiation** — to bring AC down and improve CPI. 5. **Rebaseline BAC** — if scope changes are formally approved. Since **TCPI = 1.182 > 1**, the current cost efficiency is insufficient; the project must achieve ₹1.18 of work for every ₹1 spent from now on to meet BAC — practically difficult, hence rebaselining is recommended. --- > **`[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Supplementary Set]`** **Question B (14 Marks)** **(a)** What is **Earned Value Management (EVM)**? Explain with a neat diagram how PV, EV, and AC are used in the Planning–Monitoring–Controlling cycle. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** A project has the following data: BAC = ₹50,00,000. After 6 months, PV = ₹20,00,000, EV = ₹18,00,000, AC = ₹22,00,000. Calculate all six core EVM metrics and determine the health quadrant. Suggest the role of the **Change Control Board (CCB)** in bringing the project back on track. **(7 Marks)** #### Model Solution to Question B **Part (a) — EVM Definition and Diagram:** **EVM** is a project performance measurement technique that integrates scope, schedule, and cost to assess project progress objectively. It uses three baseline values: - **PV (Planned Value)** — budgeted cost of work *scheduled*. - **EV (Earned Value)** — budgeted cost of work *actually performed*. - **AC (Actual Cost)** — real cost *incurred* for the work performed. **Diagrammatic Relationship:** ``` PMB (Performance Measurement Baseline) | | Schedule baseline defines PV over time v PV curve (S-curve over months) ---> Compared with <--- AC curve EV (a third S-curve) Deviations: CV = EV - AC (vertical gap between EV and AC) SV = EV - PV (vertical gap between EV and PV) ``` The PMB at month 6 *plans* ₹20,00,000 of work (PV). The team *performed* work worth ₹18,00,000 (EV) but *spent* ₹22,00,000 (AC). Hence the project is both behind schedule and over budget. This information flows into Controlling, where corrective actions are decided. **Part (b) — Numerical Solution:** Given: BAC = ₹50,00,000, PV = ₹20,00,000, EV = ₹18,00,000, AC = ₹22,00,000. **Step 1:** $CV = EV - AC = 18{,}00{,}000 - 22{,}00{,}000 = -4{,}00{,}000$ **Step 2:** $SV = EV - PV = 18{,}00{,}000 - 20{,}00{,}000 = -2{,}00{,}000$ **Step 3:** $CPI = \dfrac{EV}{AC} = \dfrac{18{,}00{,}000}{22{,}00{,}000} = 0.818$ **Step 4:** $SPI = \dfrac{EV}{PV} = \dfrac{18{,}00{,}000}{20{,}00{,}000} = 0.900$ **Step 5:** $EAC = \dfrac{BAC}{CPI} = \dfrac{50{,}00{,}000}{0.818} = ₹61{,}12{,}469$ **Step 6:** $VAC = BAC - EAC = 50{,}00{,}000 - 61{,}12{,}469 = -11{,}12{,}469$ **Health Quadrant:** $CPI < 1$ and $SPI < 1$ → **Critical Quadrant** (over budget, behind schedule). **Role of the Change Control Board (CCB):** The CCB is the **gatekeeper** of the project baseline. Its functions in bringing the project back on track are: 1. **Review** all change requests generated by the Controlling phase. 2. **Approve, Reject, or Defer** each request based on its impact on scope, schedule, cost, and quality. 3. **Authorize rebaselining** if the cumulative impact justifies it. 4. **Ensure traceability** through the change log. 5. **Communicate decisions** to all stakeholders for transparent alignment. In this scenario, the CCB might approve a scope-deferral change, release contingency reserves, and authorize crashing of the critical path to bring SPI above 1. --- ### 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in PMC Cycle Answers:** > 1. **Writing formulas without units** — KTU deducts 0.5 marks if "₹" or "Rupees" is missing on EAC/VAC values. > 2. **Confusing EAC formulas** — there are *three* versions of EAC; using the wrong one for the given context loses 2 marks. Always read the question's assumption. > 3. **Forgetting to interpret** — Computing CV/SV without stating *"over budget"* or *"behind schedule"* loses 1 mark. KTU wants the *meaning*, not just the number. > 4. **Mixing CPI and SPI interpretations** — CPI is about money, SPI is about time. Students often say "CPI shows we are late" — this is wrong; use SPI for time. > 5. **Omitting the loop-back in diagrams** — if you draw the PMC cycle as three separate boxes without a feedback arrow from Controlling back to Planning, you lose 1 mark in Part B(a) answers. > 6. **TCPI miscalculation** — students often write the formula upside down. Remember: numerator is **remaining budget**; denominator is **remaining cost**. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > 📌 **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Must Memorize Before Exam):** - [ ] PMC = **Planning → Monitoring → Controlling**, executed as a **closed feedback loop**. - [ ] **Planning** produces the **Performance Measurement Baseline (PMB)** containing scope + schedule + cost baselines. - [ ] **Monitoring** uses **EVM** to compute PV, EV, AC and derive CV, SV, CPI, SPI. - [ ] **Controlling** converts monitoring output into **corrective/preventive actions** via the **Change Control System**. - [ ] Memorize all **10 EVM formulas** in the exact order: CV, SV, CPI, SPI, EAC, ETC, VAC, TCPI (and the three EAC variants). - [ ] **$CV \geq 0$, $SV \geq 0$, $CPI \geq 1$, $SPI \geq 1$** → good. The **opposite** of each → bad. - [ ] **$EAC = BAC / CPI$** is the most frequently tested formula — used when "current cost performance continues." - [ ] **$TCPI > 1$** means rebaselining is likely required. - [ ] The four project-health quadrants: **Excellent, Recovering, Risky, Slow / Critical**. - [ ] Always include the **loop-back arrow** from Controlling to Planning in any diagram. - [ ] In numerical answers, end with a **"Recommended Corrective Action"** statement — KTU awards 1 mark for this conclusion. - [ ] The **Change Control Board (CCB)** is the formal authority for approving or rejecting change requests — never bypass it. - [ ] **PDCA (Plan–Do–Check–Act)** is the quality-equivalent of the PMC cycle; mention it if a question links PMC to quality management for extra marks.

topics.title: Project Control: Types of Control Processes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT863 - PROJECT MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Project Control: Types of Control Processes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## Project Control — Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Project Control** is the structured, continuous process of measuring project performance against the established baseline (scope, schedule, and cost plan), identifying variances, analyzing root causes, and initiating corrective actions to ensure the project achieves its defined objectives within approved constraints.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme terminology aligned with **PMBOK (Project Management Body of Knowledge)** and **PRINCE2** frameworks, project control is the operational heart of the **Monitoring & Controlling Process Group**. It transforms the static *project management plan* into a dynamic, responsive system.

The four universally accepted elements of any control system are:
1. **Standard / Baseline** — The reference benchmark.
2. **Sensor / Measurement** — Mechanism to capture actual data.
3. **Comparator / Analyzer** — Logic to detect deviation.
4. **Actuator / Corrector** — Authority to implement change.

## Conceptual Analogy — "The Cruise Control of a Car"

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Imagine driving a car on a highway. You set the cruise control to **100 km/h** (the **baseline**). The car's sensors continuously read the actual speed (the **measurement**). The onboard computer compares it with the set speed (the **comparison**). If you go uphill and speed drops to 90 km/h, the system increases fuel injection to bring it back to 100 km/h (the **corrective action**). Project control works identically — the project plan is the speedometer setting, and the project manager is the cruise control computer.

## The Three Pillars of Project Control

| Pillar | What is Controlled | Typical Metric |
|---|---|---|
| **Scope Control** | Deliverables, WBS integrity | Scope Variance (SV-scope) |
| **Schedule Control** | Milestones, deadlines | Schedule Variance (SV) |
| **Cost Control** | Budget, expenditures | Cost Variance (CV) |

## Types of Control Processes — The Core Classification

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 Scheme specifically classifies control processes into **three temporal categories** based on *when* the control action is applied relative to the activity execution:

### 1. Pre-Control (Feedforward / Preliminary Control)
This is **anticipatory** control applied **before** the activity begins. It focuses on inputs, prerequisites, resources, and environmental readiness to prevent problems.
- **When:** Before the work package starts.
- **Focus:** Inputs → Resources → Plans → Policies.
- **KTU Keyword:** *Anticipatory / Preventive Control*.

### 2. Concurrent Control (Steering / Real-time / "Walk-through" Control)
This is **live monitoring** that occurs **during** execution. The project manager supervises ongoing work and makes in-flight adjustments.
- **When:** While the activity is in progress.
- **Focus:** Real-time dashboards, daily stand-ups, Gantt updates.
- **KTU Keyword:** *Steering Control*.

### 3. Post-Control (Feedback / Output Control)
This is **retrospective** analysis conducted **after** an activity or phase is complete. Lessons learned feed forward into future phases or projects.
- **When:** After the deliverable / milestone is completed.
- **Focus:** Variance analysis, root-cause analysis, lessons learned.
- **KTU Keyword:** *Feedback Control*.

> [!NOTE]
> **Memory Trick for Students:** **P**revent → **C**orrect **N**ow → **L**earn **L**ater = **PCNL** (Pre-Control, Concurrent, Later-control). Or simply: *Before, During, After* — a universal life skill that applies to project management too.

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Detailed Breakdown of the Three Control Types

### A. Pre-Control (Feedforward Control)

**Logic Steps (Why & How):**
- **Why use it?** Because the cost of preventing a defect is **5–10 times lower** than the cost of correcting it after the fact (a principle from Quality Management — *Deming's Chain Reaction*).
- **How does it work?** It establishes strict **gating criteria** that must be satisfied before work begins. Example: A design cannot move to "Approved for Construction" until the design review checklist is 100% green.

**Inputs Controlled:**
- Scope statement clarity
- Resource availability (manpower, machinery, materials)
- Budget approvals
- Stakeholder sign-offs
- Risk register and mitigation plans
- Regulatory and compliance clearances

**Tools Used:**
- Gate reviews / Stage-gate process
- Readiness checklists
- Feasibility studies
- Work Authorization System

### B. Concurrent Control (Steering Control)

**Logic Steps (Why & How):**
- **Why use it?** Real-world projects rarely follow the plan linearly. Scope creep, resource conflicts, and external dependencies cause deviations every week.
- **How does it work?** Concurrent control operates on the principle of **immediate feedback loops**. As work is being done, performance data is captured and analyzed. If the deviation exceeds a pre-agreed **threshold**, a corrective action is triggered **without waiting for completion**.

**Mechanisms:**
- Daily stand-up meetings (Agile/Scrum)
- Weekly progress reviews (PRINCE2 / Traditional)
- Earned Value Analysis (monthly)
- Change advisory board (CAB) reviews
- Issue logs and risk re-assessment

### C. Post-Control (Feedback Control)

**Logic Steps (Why & How):**
- **Why use it?** Even with perfect pre- and concurrent control, history must be analyzed to improve future performance. It answers: *"What actually happened vs. what we planned?"*
- **How does it work?** Historical data from completed phases is reviewed. Variances are calculated, root causes determined, and **lessons learned** are documented for organizational learning.

**Activities:**
- Post-project reviews (often called *Project Closure Reports*)
- Earned Value retrospective
- Cost and schedule variance analysis
- Customer satisfaction surveys
- Lessons-learned repository updates

## 2.2 Comparison Matrix of the Three Control Types

| Parameter | Pre-Control | Concurrent Control | Post-Control |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Timing** | Before work starts | During execution | After completion |
| **Alternative Name** | Feedforward | Steering / Real-time | Feedback |
| **Primary Aim** | Prevent deviations | Detect and correct deviations | Learn from deviations |
| **Information Source** | Plans, baselines, forecasts | Live performance data | Historical records, archives |
| **Cost of Correction** | **Lowest** | Moderate | **Highest** |
| **Typical Tool** | Stage-gate reviews | Dashboards, EVM | Variance analysis, PCR |
| **Decision Speed** | Slow (gating) | **Fast** (real-time) | Slow (analytical) |
| **Responsibility** | Sponsor / PMO | Project Manager | PMO / OPM |
| **PMBOK Mapping** | Planning Process Group | Monitoring & Controlling | Closing Process Group |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield, Board-Exam Essential)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following formulas are **directly examinable** in KTU 2024 Scheme. Memorize the variables and units.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{CV (Cost Variance)} &= \text{EV} - \text{AC} \\
\text{SV (Schedule Variance)} &= \text{EV} - \text{PV} \\
\text{CPI (Cost Performance Index)} &= \frac{\text{EV}}{\text{AC}} \\
\text{SPI (Schedule Performance Index)} &= \frac{\text{EV}}{\text{PV}} \\
\text{EAC (Estimate at Completion)} &= \frac{\text{BAC}}{\text{CPI}} \\
\text{VAC (Variance at Completion)} &= \text{BAC} - \text{EAC} \\
\text{TCPI (To-Complete Performance Index)} &= \frac{\text{BAC} - \text{EV}}{\text{BAC} - \text{AC}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Variable Legend (in standard currency units, e.g., ₹ Lakhs):**

| Variable | Meaning | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| **EV** | Earned Value (work actually completed × planned rate) | ₹ |
| **PV** | Planned Value (budgeted cost of work scheduled) | ₹ |
| **AC** | Actual Cost (real money spent) | ₹ |
| **BAC** | Budget at Completion (total approved budget) | ₹ |
| **CV** | Cost Variance | ₹ |
| **SV** | Schedule Variance | ₹ |
| **CPI** | Cost Performance Index | Ratio (≥ 1 = good) |
| **SPI** | Schedule Performance Index | Ratio (≥ 1 = good) |
| **EAC** | Estimate at Completion | ₹ |
| **VAC** | Variance at Completion | ₹ |
| **TCPI** | To-Complete Performance Index | Ratio |

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation Rule:** If **CV > 0** → Under budget (good). If **CV < 0** → Over budget (bad). Same polarity logic applies to SV. CPI and SPI work inversely: **CPI < 1** = bad, **CPI > 1** = good.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Construction Industry:** Pre-control ensures soil testing and permits are clear *before* excavation; concurrent control uses daily site reports; post-control uses the final project audit.
- **Software Industry:** Pre-control = design review; concurrent control = continuous integration dashboards; post-control = sprint retrospectives.
- **Manufacturing:** Pre-control = quality of raw materials; concurrent control = in-process inspection; post-control = final acceptance testing.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Numerical Problem (KTU Board Exam Standard)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Statement:** A project has a **BAC = ₹ 2,00,000**. At the end of Month 6, the project status is as follows:
> - **PV (Planned Value)** = ₹ 1,00,000
> - **EV (Earned Value)** = ₹ 90,000
> - **AC (Actual Cost)** = ₹ 1,10,000
>
> Calculate **CV, SV, CPI, SPI, EAC, VAC, and TCPI**. Interpret each.

### Step 1: Calculate Cost Variance (CV)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{CV} &= \text{EV} - \text{AC} \\
\text{CV} &= 90{,}000 - 1{,}10{,}000 \\
\text{CV} &= -20{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic:** We subtract Actual Cost from Earned Value because CV = value of work done minus money actually spent.
**Interpretation:** CV is **negative**, meaning the project is **over budget by ₹ 20,000**. This is a **red flag** triggering cost-control corrective action.

### Step 2: Calculate Schedule Variance (SV)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{SV} &= \text{EV} - \text{PV} \\
\text{SV} &= 90{,}000 - 1{,}00{,}000 \\
\text{SV} &= -10{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic:** SV compares work actually accomplished (EV) against work scheduled (PV).
**Interpretation:** SV is **negative**, meaning the project is **behind schedule by ₹ 10,000 worth of work**.

### Step 3: Calculate Cost Performance Index (CPI)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{CPI} &= \frac{\text{EV}}{\text{AC}} \\
\text{CPI} &= \frac{90{,}000}{1{,}10{,}000} \\
\text{CPI} &= 0.818
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic:** CPI tells us the *efficiency* of spending — for every ₹1 spent, we earned ₹0.818 of value.
**Interpretation:** **CPI < 1** → inefficient cost performance. The project is getting **less value per rupee** than planned.

### Step 4: Calculate Schedule Performance Index (SPI)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{SPI} &= \frac{\text{EV}}{\text{PV}} \\
\text{SPI} &= \frac{90{,}000}{1{,}00{,}000} \\
\text{SPI} &= 0.90
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic:** SPI < 1 means we are progressing slower than planned.
**Interpretation:** Only **90%** of the planned schedule progress has been achieved.

### Step 5: Calculate Estimate at Completion (EAC)

Using the standard formula $EAC = BAC / CPI$ (assuming current CPI continues):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{EAC} &= \frac{\text{BAC}}{\text{CPI}} \\
\text{EAC} &= \frac{2{,}00{,}000}{0.818} \\
\text{EAC} &= 2{,}44{,}499 \text{ (approx.)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** If the current inefficient trend continues, the project will **cost ₹ 2.44 Lakhs** instead of the planned ₹ 2 Lakhs — a **22% budget overrun**.

### Step 6: Calculate Variance at Completion (VAC)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{VAC} &= \text{BAC} - \text{EAC} \\
\text{VAC} &= 2{,}00{,}000 - 2{,}44{,}499 \\
\text{VAC} &= -44{,}499
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** A negative VAC of ₹ 44,499 represents the **expected overrun** at project completion if no corrective action is taken.

### Step 7: Calculate To-Complete Performance Index (TCPI)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{TCPI} &= \frac{\text{BAC} - \text{EV}}{\text{BAC} - \text{AC}} \\
\text{TCPI} &= \frac{2{,}00{,}000 - 90{,}000}{2{,}00{,}000 - 1{,}10{,}000} \\
\text{TCPI} &= \frac{1{,}10{,}000}{90{,}000} \\
\text{TCPI} &= 1.222
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic:** TCPI tells the project manager the *required efficiency* for the **remaining work** to complete the project on the original BAC.
**Interpretation:** To recover and finish within budget, the team must achieve **TCPI = 1.222**, i.e., for every ₹1 spent from now on, ₹1.222 of value must be delivered. Since this is **higher than 1**, it is challenging but achievable.

### Step 8: Consolidated Analytical Matrix

| Metric | Value | Status | Triggered Action |
|---|---|---|---|
| **CV** | −₹ 20,000 | Over budget | Cost-reduction review |
| **SV** | −₹ 10,000 | Behind schedule | Fast-tracking / crashing |
| **CPI** | 0.818 | Inefficient | Renegotiate vendor rates |
| **SPI** | 0.90 | Lagging | Add resources, parallel tasks |
| **EAC** | ₹ 2,44,499 | Over BAC | Sponsor re-baseline discussion |
| **VAC** | −₹ 44,499 | Anticipated overrun | Reserve fund release |
| **TCPI** | 1.222 | Challenging | Performance improvement plan |

## 3.2 Decision Matrix — Which Control Process To Use When

| Situation Detected | Optimal Control Type | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| Unclear stakeholder requirements at project start | **Pre-Control** | Resolve ambiguity *before* WBS is frozen |
| Construction work is 30% behind schedule mid-way | **Concurrent Control** | Real-time crashing or fast-tracking is needed |
| Defective product discovered after delivery | **Post-Control** | Root-cause analysis + lessons learned |
| Vendor selection at procurement stage | **Pre-Control** | Vetting happens *before* contract award |
| Daily burn-rate of budget exceeds plan | **Concurrent Control** | Stop the bleed immediately |
| End-of-phase milestone missed | **Post-Control** | Analyze for next-phase recovery |
| Risk register update before major go-live | **Pre-Control** | Mitigate *before* cutover |
