# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Integrated Project Control Loop (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Project Charter and Baseline] --> B[Stage Gate Review]
    B -->|Approved| C[Work Authorization Issued]
    B -->|Rejected| A
    C --> D[Execution of Work Packages]
    D --> E[Real Time Data Capture]
    E --> F{EVM Analysis and Variance Check}
    F -->|Within Threshold| G[Continue Execution]
    F -->|Exceeds Threshold| H[Trigger Corrective Action]
    H --> I[Change Control Board Review]
    I -->|Approved| J[Update Baseline]
    I -->|Rejected| K[Roll Back to Last Good State]
    J --> D
    G --> L[Work Package Complete]
    K --> D
    L --> M[Post Control and Lessons Learned]
    M --> N[Project Archive and Closure Report]
    N --> O[Update Organizational Knowledge Base]
    O --> A
```

**How to read this diagram:**
- The **outer loop** (A → B → C → D) represents **Pre-Control**.
- The **inner loop** (D → E → F → H → I → J → D) represents **Concurrent Control**.
- The **terminal block** (L → M → N → O) represents **Post-Control**, which feeds back into the start of the *next* project.

## 4.2 Three-Layer Control Architecture (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    L1[Layer 1: Pre Control Inputs] --> L2[Layer 2: Concurrent Monitoring]
    L2 --> L3[Layer 3: Post Control Feedback]
    L3 -.Lessons Learned.-> L1

    subgraph INPUT_BLOCK[Pre Control Inputs]
        I1[Scope Baseline]
        I2[Schedule Baseline]
        I3[Cost Baseline]
        I4[Risk Register]
    end

    subgraph MONITORING_BLOCK[Concurrent Monitoring]
        M1[Daily Progress Reports]
        M2[Earned Value Tracking]
        M3[Issue and Risk Logs]
        M4[Quality Audits]
    end

    subgraph FEEDBACK_BLOCK[Post Control Feedback]
        F1[Variance Analysis Report]
        F2[Root Cause Analysis]
        F3[Lessons Learned Database]
        F4[Project Closure Report]
    end

    I1 --> M1
    I2 --> M2
    I3 --> M2
    I4 --> M3

    M1 --> F1
    M2 --> F1
    M3 --> F2
    M4 --> F2

    F1 --> F3
    F2 --> F3
    F3 --> F4
```

## 4.3 Decision Tree for Control Process Selection (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([Project Event Detected]) --> Q1{When did the event occur?}
    Q1 -->|Before work starts| P1[Apply Pre Control]
    Q1 -->|During work| P2[Apply Concurrent Control]
    Q1 -->|After work done| P3[Apply Post Control]

    P1 --> A1[Update Baselines]
    P1 --> A2[Authorize Work]

    P2 --> B1{EVM Variance Check}
    B1 -->|Within 10 percent| B2[Continue and Log]
    B1 -->|Exceeds 10 percent| B3[Corrective Action]

    P3 --> C1[Perform Variance Analysis]
    C1 --> C2[Document Lessons Learned]
    C2 --> C3[Update Knowledge Base]

    A2 --> End([Baseline Ready])
    B2 --> End2([Steady State])
    B3 --> End2
    C3 --> End3([Organizational Learning])
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Differentiate clearly between Pre-Control, Concurrent Control, and Post-Control. Give one engineering example for each.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks Allocation):**

| Control Type | Timing | Engineering Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Pre-Control** | Before work begins | Soil testing and statutory approvals before starting bridge construction |
| **Concurrent Control** | During execution | Real-time monitoring of concrete curing temperature during dam construction |
| **Post-Control** | After completion | Post-commissioning vibration analysis of a turbine to capture lessons for the next unit |

(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for timing distinction, 1 mark for the engineering example.)

---

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**What is the significance of the Cost Performance Index (CPI) and Schedule Performance Index (SPI) in project control? Interpret the meaning if CPI = 0.85 and SPI = 1.10.**

**Model Answer:**
CPI measures the **cost efficiency** of work performed; SPI measures the **schedule efficiency**.
- $CPI = \dfrac{EV}{AC}$
- $SPI = \dfrac{EV}{PV}$

Given **CPI = 0.85** and **SPI = 1.10**:
- CPI = 0.85 → for every ₹1 spent, only ₹0.85 of work was earned. **Project is over budget.**
- SPI = 1.10 → for every ₹1 of work scheduled, ₹1.10 was earned. **Project is ahead of schedule.**

**Interpretation:** The project is finishing work faster than planned but at a higher cost — a classic *crashing* scenario where additional resources are accelerating delivery.

(1 mark for formula, 1 mark for CPI interpretation, 1 mark for SPI interpretation.)

---

## PART B — Full-Length 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - Students frequently confuse **SV (Schedule Variance)** sign interpretation. Always remember: **EV − PV**, not PV − EV.
> - Many candidates write *Earned Value* and *Planned Value* definitions incorrectly. **EV = work actually completed**, **PV = work scheduled**. Do not interchange.
> - In TCPI derivation, students often forget to subtract already-earned value from BAC. Marks are deducted for **BAC − EV** being written as just BAC.
> - Always show **units (₹, %, days)** in the final answer — KTU examiners award 0.5 marks specifically for units.
> - For 14-mark questions, **diagrams earn marks**. Always draw at least one Mermaid/hand-drawn flow.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]

**Part (a) [7 Marks]:** Explain in detail the **three types of project control processes**. Compare them with a suitable tabular column and a flow diagram.

**Part (b) [7 Marks]:** A project has the following data:
- **BAC = ₹ 5,00,000**
- After 6 months: **PV = ₹ 2,50,000, EV = ₹ 2,00,000, AC = ₹ 2,80,000**
- Calculate **CV, SV, CPI, SPI, EAC, VAC, and TCPI**. Comment on the project health.

---

### SOLUTION TO QUESTION A

#### Part (a) — Model Answer [7 Marks]

**Introduction (1 mark):**
Project control is the application of the *plan–do–check–act* cycle to a project. The three temporal types — pre-control, concurrent control, and post-control — correspond to the *plan*, *do/check*, and *act/learn* phases respectively.

**Detailed Explanation (4 marks):**

1. **Pre-Control (Feedforward):**
   - Applied *before* an activity begins.
   - Aims to *prevent* deviations by ensuring inputs (resources, approvals, designs) are ready.
   - Tools: Stage-gate reviews, work authorization systems, readiness checklists.
   - Example: Conducting a design freeze review before issuing a fabrication drawing.

2. **Concurrent Control (Steering):**
   - Applied *during* execution.
   - Aims to *detect and correct* deviations in real time.
   - Tools: Earned Value Management, daily stand-ups, change control systems.
   - Example: Monitoring daily concrete pours against the pour schedule.

3. **Post-Control (Feedback):**
   - Applied *after* an activity is completed.
   - Aims to *learn* from deviations to improve future phases/projects.
   - Tools: Variance analysis, post-project reviews, lessons-learned database.
   - Example: Conducting a project closure audit after plant commissioning.

**Comparison Table (1 mark):**

| Parameter | Pre-Control | Concurrent Control | Post-Control |
|---|---|---|---|
| Timing | Before | During | After |
| Goal | Prevent | Detect & Correct | Learn |
| Cost to Fix | Lowest | Medium | Highest |
| Example | Stage gate | EVM | Lessons learned |

**Flow Diagram (1 mark):** The student should reproduce the three-layer control loop diagram from Section 4.2.

---

#### Part (b) — Model Answer [7 Marks]

**Given:**
$BAC = 5{,}00{,}000$, $PV = 2{,}50{,}000$, $EV = 2{,}00{,}000$, $AC = 2{,}80{,}000$.

**Step 1 — Cost Variance (CV):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
CV &= EV - AC \\
CV &= 2{,}00{,}000 - 2{,}80{,}000 \\
CV &= -80{,}000 \text{ ₹}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Schedule Variance (SV):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
SV &= EV - PV \\
SV &= 2{,}00{,}000 - 2{,}50{,}000 \\
SV &= -50{,}000 \text{ ₹}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — CPI:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
CPI &= \frac{EV}{AC} = \frac{2{,}00{,}000}{2{,}80{,}000} = 0.714
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — SPI:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
SPI &= \frac{EV}{PV} = \frac{2{,}00{,}000}{2{,}50{,}000} = 0.80
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — EAC:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
EAC &= \frac{BAC}{CPI} = \frac{5{,}00{,}000}{0.714} = 7{,}00{,}280 \text{ ₹}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — VAC:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
VAC &= BAC - EAC = 5{,}00{,}000 - 7{,}00{,}280 = -2{,}00{,}280 \text{ ₹}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7 — TCPI:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
TCPI &= \frac{BAC - EV}{BAC - AC} = \frac{5{,}00{,}000 - 2{,}00{,}000}{5{,}00{,}000 - 2{,}80{,}000} \\
TCPI &= \frac{3{,}00{,}000}{2{,}20{,}000} = 1.364
\end{aligned}
$$

**Comment on Project Health (1 mark):**
Both CV and SV are negative, indicating the project is **over budget and behind schedule**. The CPI of 0.714 and SPI of 0.80 confirm poor performance on both dimensions. EAC shows a forecasted **40% budget overrun**. The required TCPI of 1.364 is challenging, and **immediate corrective action** through concurrent control is mandatory.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam — May 2023]

**Part (a) [7 Marks]:** Describe the **Earned Value Management (EVM)** system as a project control tool. List its key performance indicators and explain how they help in concurrent control.

**Part (b) [7 Marks]:** Explain the **Work Authorization System** and the **Change Control System** as two essential mechanisms of pre-control and concurrent control respectively.

---

### SOLUTION TO QUESTION B

#### Part (a) — Model Answer [7 Marks]

**Definition (1 mark):**
Earned Value Management is an integrated project control methodology that combines scope, schedule, and cost measurements to assess project progress and performance objectively.

**Core PV, EV, AC Definitions (2 marks):**
- **PV (Planned Value):** Authorized budget assigned to scheduled work.
- **EV (Earned Value):** Measure of work performed expressed in terms of the budget authorized for that work.
- **AC (Actual Cost):** Realized cost incurred for the work accomplished.

**Key Performance Indicators (3 marks):**
1. **CV = EV − AC** → cost variance in currency.
2. **SV = EV − PV** → schedule variance in currency.
3. **CPI = EV / AC** → cost efficiency ratio.
4. **SPI = EV / PV** → schedule efficiency ratio.
5. **EAC, VAC, TCPI** → forecasting indicators.

**Role in Concurrent Control (1 mark):**
EVM provides **real-time visibility** into project health by converting schedule and cost performance into quantifiable metrics. When CPI or SPI crosses pre-defined thresholds, the project manager triggers corrective action immediately — making EVM the **most powerful concurrent control tool**.

---

#### Part (b) — Model Answer [7 Marks]

**Work Authorization System — Pre-Control Mechanism (3.5 marks):**
The Work Authorization System is a formal procedure that ensures work packages are started **only after** they are officially approved by the project manager or PMO. It is a **pre-control mechanism** because it acts as a gatekeeper:
- Prevents unauthorized or premature work.
- Ensures resources, designs, and dependencies are ready.
- Maintains the integrity of the cost baseline.
- **Example:** In a construction project, excavation is authorized only after soil test reports and statutory permits are approved.

**Change Control System — Concurrent Control Mechanism (3.5 marks):**
The Change Control System is a structured process for managing changes to project baselines. It is a **concurrent control mechanism** because it operates in real time:
- All change requests must flow through the Change Control Board (CCB).
- Impact analysis (cost, schedule, scope, quality, risk) is mandatory.
- Approved changes update the baseline; rejected changes are archived with reasons.
- **Example:** During software development, a request to add a new module mid-sprint is reviewed by the CCB before being merged.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist (Last-Night-Revision Friendly)**

- ✅ **Project Control =** Plan + Measure + Compare + Correct — repeated continuously.
- ✅ **Three Types:** Pre-Control (Before), Concurrent (During), Post-Control (After) — *PCN-L mnemonic*.
- ✅ **Pre-Control tools:** Stage-gate reviews, Work Authorization System, readiness checklists, feasibility studies.
- ✅ **Concurrent Control tools:** Earned Value Management, daily stand-ups, Change Control System, dashboards.
- ✅ **Post-Control tools:** Variance analysis, root-cause analysis, lessons learned, project closure report.
- ✅ **The 7 Key EVM Formulas:** CV, SV, CPI, SPI, EAC, VAC, TCPI — must be memorized *with signs*.
- ✅ **Golden Rule:** EV is the *central* metric — both CV and SV use EV.
- ✅ **Sign Convention:** Negative CV / SV = trouble. CPI / SPI > 1 = good, < 1 = bad.
- ✅ **TCPI Meaning:** It is the *future performance efficiency* required to recover the project.
- ✅ **Cost of Correction Ascends:** Pre-Control < Concurrent Control < Post-Control.
- ✅ **KTU 2024 Mapping:** This topic tests CO3 (Monitor and Control Project) under *Apply* and *Analyze* levels of Bloom's Taxonomy.
- ✅ **Examiner Loves:** Definitions, comparison tables, EVM numericals, and at least one diagram.
- ✅ **Common Pitfall:** Writing *Planned Value* as "value of work actually completed" — wrong; that is *Earned Value*. PV is **what was planned**, EV is **what was achieved**.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Design of Control Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT863 - PROJECT MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Design of Control Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Project Control System** is an integrated, structured framework comprising policies, procedures, processes, tools, and metrics that project managers deploy to **monitor, measure, compare, and regulate** project performance against the approved **Performance Measurement Baseline (PMB)**. According to the **PMBOK® Guide (7th Edition)** and KTU's Project Management syllabus, the design of such a control system involves architecting the mechanisms for **scope control, schedule control, cost control, quality control, risk control, and change control**.

The system fundamentally rests on three pillars:

1. **Baseline Establishment** — Defining the reference plan (Scope, Schedule, Cost baselines).
2. **Performance Measurement** — Continuous data collection and Earned Value Analysis.
3. **Corrective Action Mechanism** — Variance analysis leading to integrated change control.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The control system is *not* reactive firefighting — it is a **proactively engineered** architecture that anticipates deviation, defines thresholds, and prescribes corrective actions **before** performance breaches the baseline.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Cruise Control System

Imagine driving a car equipped with **cruise control**:

| Cruise Control Component | Equivalent in Project Control System |
|---|---|
| **Set Speed (e.g., 80 km/h)** | Performance Measurement Baseline (Planned Value) |
| **Actual Speed Reading** | Earned Value (EV) / Actual Cost (AC) |
| **Dashboard Display** | Status Reports, Dashboards, KPIs |
| **Throttle Adjustment** | Corrective / Preventive Actions |
| **Driver Override (Brake)** | Change Control Board (CCB) Decisions |
| **Speed Sensor** | Work Performance Information System |

When the car climbs a hill, speed drops — the system *automatically* increases fuel. When it descends, it cuts fuel. Similarly, the **project control system** continuously compares actuals against the plan and triggers **corrective actions** when **Cost Variance (CV)** or **Schedule Variance (SV)** crosses the pre-defined control limits. The project manager acts as the "driver" — interpreting sensor data and overriding when strategic re-planning is required.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Takeaway:** A well-designed control system makes deviation **visible, measurable, and actionable**. Without it, the project runs "blind" — like a car without a speedometer or fuel gauge.

---

## 1.3 Key Physical / Standard Metrics

In project control, the following **standard metrics** are universally accepted:

- **CPI (Cost Performance Index):** *Target value = **1.0*** (on budget)
- **SPI (Schedule Performance Index):** *Target value = **1.0*** (on schedule)
- **Control Limits:** *Upper Control Limit (UCL) = μ + **3σ***; *Lower Control Limit (LCL) = μ − **3σ*** (Three-Sigma Rule)
- **Baseline Freeze Point:** *Time = **0** at project authorization*

---

## 1.4 Scope of a Project Control System

The system must address the **seven control dimensions** recognized by KTU's outcome-based framework:

1. **Scope Control** — Preventing uncontrolled scope creep.
2. **Schedule Control** — Time-based performance tracking.
3. **Cost Control** — Earned Value & financial variance tracking.
4. **Quality Control** — Statistical Process Control (SPC) via control charts.
5. **Risk Control** — Risk register updates, residual risk monitoring.
6. **Change Control** — Configuration & change management protocols.
7. **Communication Control** — Information distribution & performance reporting.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Architecture of a Project Control System — Layered Breakdown

A robust control system is designed in **five logical layers**:

### Layer 1: Baseline Layer (Foundation)
- **Scope Baseline:** WBS + WBS Dictionary + Scope Statement
- **Schedule Baseline:** Approved Gantt / Network Diagram
- **Cost Baseline:** Time-phased budget (S-Curve)

### Layer 2: Measurement Layer (Sensors)
- Earned Value computations: **PV, EV, AC**
- Work Performance Data collection (timesheets, cost ledgers)
- Quality inspection data (defects, rework rates)

### Layer 3: Analysis Layer (Brain)
- Variance Analysis: **CV = EV − AC**, **SV = EV − PV**
- Index Computation: **CPI = EV / AC**, **SPI = EV / PV**
- Forecasting: **EAC, ETC, VAC, TCPI**
- Statistical Process Control (SPC): Control Charts

### Layer 4: Decision Layer (Control)
- Variance threshold rules (e.g., trigger action if |CV| > 10% of budget)
- Corrective Action Selection
- Change Request Generation
- Risk Re-assessment

### Layer 5: Action Layer (Actuator)
- Implement Corrective Actions
- Update Project Management Plan
- Update Risk Register
- Communicate to Stakeholders

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Insight:** This five-layer architecture mirrors the **Plan-Do-Check-Act (PDCA)** cycle of Deming, allowing the system to be **iterative** and **self-correcting**.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

The following table consolidates **all critical formulas** for designing and operating a project control system.

### 2.2.1 Earned Value Management (EVM) Formulas

| # | Metric | Formula | Interpretation | Target |
|---|--------|---------|----------------|--------|
| 1 | Planned Value (PV) | $\text{PV} = \text{Budget} \times \text{\% Planned}$ | Authorized budget scheduled | — |
| 2 | Earned Value (EV) | $\text{EV} = \text{Budget} \times \text{\% Completed}$ | Value of work actually done | — |
| 3 | Actual Cost (AC) | Sum of incurred costs | Real money spent | — |
| 4 | Cost Variance (CV) | $\text{CV} = \text{EV} - \text{AC}$ | $>0$ = Under budget | $\geq 0$ |
| 5 | Schedule Variance (SV) | $\text{SV} = \text{EV} - \text{PV}$ | $>0$ = Ahead of schedule | $\geq 0$ |
| 6 | Cost Performance Index (CPI) | $\text{CPI} = \frac{\text{EV}}{\text{AC}}$ | Efficiency of cost usage | $= 1.0$ |
| 7 | Schedule Performance Index (SPI) | $\text{SPI} = \frac{\text{EV}}{\text{PV}}$ | Efficiency of time usage | $= 1.0$ |
| 8 | Estimate at Completion (EAC) | $\text{EAC} = \frac{\text{BAC}}{\text{CPI}}$ | Forecasted total cost | — |
| 9 | Estimate to Complete (ETC) | $\text{ETC} = \text{EAC} - \text{AC}$ | Remaining cost to finish | — |
| 10 | Variance at Completion (VAC) | $\text{VAC} = \text{BAC} - \text{EAC}$ | Forecasted cost overrun | $\geq 0$ |
| 11 | To-Complete Performance Index (TCPI) | $\text{TCPI} = \frac{\text{BAC} - \text{EV}}{\text{BAC} - \text{AC}}$ | Required CPI to finish on budget | — |
| 12 | Percent Spent (PS) | $\text{PS} = \frac{\text{AC}}{\text{BAC}}$ | Budget consumed | — |
| 13 | Percent Complete (PC) | $\text{PC} = \frac{\text{EV}}{\text{BAC}}$ | Work value earned | — |

### 2.2.2 Statistical Process Control (SPC) — Control Chart Formulas

| # | Chart Type | Center Line (CL) | Upper Control Limit (UCL) | Lower Control Limit (LCL) |
|---|-----------|------------------|---------------------------|---------------------------|
| 14 | Mean Chart (X-bar) | $\bar{\bar{X}}$ | $\bar{\bar{X}} + A_2 \bar{R}$ | $\bar{\bar{X}} - A_2 \bar{R}$ |
| 15 | Range Chart (R) | $\bar{R}$ | $D_4 \bar{R}$ | $D_3 \bar{R}$ |
| 16 | p-Chart (Defects Proportion) | $\bar{p}$ | $\bar{p} + 3\sqrt{\frac{\bar{p}(1-\bar{p})}{n}}$ | $\bar{p} - 3\sqrt{\frac{\bar{p}(1-\bar{p})}{n}}$ |
| 17 | c-Chart (Defects per Unit) | $\bar{c}$ | $\bar{c} + 3\sqrt{\bar{c}}$ | $\bar{c} - 3\sqrt{\bar{c}}$ |
| 18 | u-Chart (Defects per Sample) | $\bar{u}$ | $\bar{u} + 3\sqrt{\frac{\bar{u}}{n}}$ | $\bar{u} - 3\sqrt{\frac{\bar{u}}{n}}$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Three-Sigma Rule:** Control limits are set at **±3σ** from the centerline, corresponding to **99.73%** confidence interval under the normal distribution. Points falling outside these limits signal **out-of-control** conditions.

### 2.2.3 Variance & Threshold Rules

| # | Rule | Formula | Trigger Condition |
|---|------|---------|-------------------|
| 19 | Cost Variance % | $\text{CV\%} = \frac{\text{CV}}{\text{EV}} \times 100$ | $\vert \text{CV\%} \vert > 10\%$ → Action |
| 20 | Schedule Variance % | $\text{SV\%} = \frac{\text{SV}}{\text{PV}} \times 100$ | $\vert \text{SV\%} \vert > 10\%$ → Action |

### 2.2.4 S-Curve (Cumulative Cost Curve) Equations

| # | Element | Formula |
|---|---------|---------|
| 21 | Cumulative PV (BCWS) | $\sum \text{PV}_i$ over time |
| 22 | Cumulative EV (BCWP) | $\sum \text{EV}_i$ over time |
| 23 | Cumulative AC (ACWP) | $\sum \text{AC}_i$ over time |
| 24 | Schedule Slip (Days) | $\text{Slip} = \frac{\text{SV}}{\text{Daily PV Rate}}$ |

---

## 2.3 Real-World Utility in Engineering Fields

- **Construction Industry:** Earned Value + S-Curve tracking on-site for mega-projects (e.g., Kochi Metro Rail — used EVM for phase-wise cost control).
- **Software Engineering:** TCPI used to forecast "effort" needed to complete agile sprints within sprint capacity.
- **Manufacturing:** SPC p-charts monitor defect rates across production lines; used by Toyota, Boeing.
- **R&D Projects:** EVMS mandated by the **U.S. Department of Defense** for all defense contracts above $20M.
- **Civil Engineering:** Gantt + EVM for bridge/highway construction under EPC contracts.

> [!NOTE]
> The control system bridges **strategy (baseline)** with **execution (actuals)** — without it, even well-planned projects fail due to "drift blindness."

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Cost Performance Index (CPI) from Cost Variance

The **Cost Variance (CV)** is the *absolute* deviation between value earned and cost spent. To obtain an *efficiency ratio* that is **dimensionless** and **scale-independent**, we normalize CV by AC:

$$
\text{CV} = \text{EV} - \text{AC}
$$

Dividing both sides by AC:

$$
\frac{\text{CV}}{\text{AC}} = \frac{\text{EV}}{\text{AC}} - \frac{\text{AC}}{\text{AC}}
$$

This simplifies to:

$$
\frac{\text{CV}}{\text{AC}} = \frac{\text{EV}}{\text{AC}} - 1
$$

By definition, the **Cost Performance Index (CPI)** is the ratio of work performed to money spent:

$$
\text{CPI} = \frac{\text{EV}}{\text{AC}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why dimensionless?** EV is measured in currency (₹/$), and AC is also in currency. The ratio **₹/₹ = 1** (dimensionless), making CPI comparable across projects of any size.

**Interpretation Rule:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{CPI} > 1.0 &\implies \text{Under Budget (favorable)} \\
\text{CPI} = 1.0 &\implies \text{On Budget} \\
\text{CPI} < 1.0 &\implies \text{Over Budget (unfavorable)}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation: To-Complete Performance Index (TCPI)

TCPI answers the critical question: **"What efficiency must we maintain from now onwards to finish on the original budget?"**

The remaining work value to be earned is:

$$
\text{Remaining Work} = \text{BAC} - \text{EV}
$$

The remaining budget available is:

$$
\text{Remaining Budget} = \text{BAC} - \text{AC}
$$

The ratio of these gives the **required efficiency going forward**:

$$
\text{TCPI} = \frac{\text{BAC} - \text{EV}}{\text{BAC} - \text{AC}}
$$

**Worked Example — TCPI Calculation:**

> A project has $\text{BAC} = ₹10{,}00{,}000$. At 50% completion, $\text{EV} = ₹5{,}00{,}000$ and $\text{AC} = ₹6{,}00{,}000$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{TCPI} &= \frac{10{,}00{,}000 - 5{,}00{,}000}{10{,}00{,}000 - 6{,}00{,}000} \\
&= \frac{5{,}00{,}000}{4{,}00{,}000} \\
&= 1.25
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** The remaining work of ₹5,00,000 must be completed with only ₹4,00,000 budget — requiring a **CPI of 1.25** (i.e., 25% more efficiency than planned) to finish on budget. If 1.25 is unattainable, the manager must request a budget revision.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: EAC (Estimate at Completion) under Different Scenarios

The **EAC** forecast depends on the assumption about future performance.

### Scenario 1: Past performance will continue unchanged (most common)

$$
\text{EAC} = \frac{\text{BAC}}{\text{CPI}}
$$

**Derivation logic:** If we've spent ₹1 to earn ₹0.80 (CPI = 0.80), then to earn the full BAC, we need BAC / 0.80 rupees.

### Scenario 2: Past performance + new estimate for remaining work

$$
\text{EAC} = \text{AC} + \text{ETC}
$$

### Scenario 3: Both past performance and new estimate considered

$$
\text{EAC} = \text{AC} + \frac{\text{BAC} - \text{EV}}{\text{CPI} \times \text{SPI}}
$$

This assumes both schedule and cost performance will persist, multiplying CPI × SPI to penalize the forecast.

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example — Full EVM Cycle

> **Project Data:** A construction project has **BAC = ₹20,00,000**, scheduled duration **10 months**. At the **5-month mark**:
> - Work completed: **40%**
> - Actual cost incurred: **₹10,00,000**
> - Budget planned for 5 months: **45% of BAC = ₹9,00,000**

### Step 1: Compute PV (Planned Value)

$$
\text{PV} = 0.45 \times 20{,}00{,}000 = ₹9{,}00{,}000
$$

### Step 2: Compute EV (Earned Value)

$$
\text{EV} = 0.40 \times 20{,}00{,}000 = ₹8{,}00{,}000
$$

### Step 3: AC is given

$$
\text{AC} = ₹10{,}00{,}000
$$

### Step 4: Cost Variance

$$
\text{CV} = \text{EV} - \text{AC} = 8{,}00{,}000 - 10{,}00{,}000 = -₹2{,}00{,}000
$$

### Step 5: Schedule Variance

$$
\text{SV} = \text{EV} - \text{PV} = 8{,}00{,}000 - 9{,}00{,}000 = -₹1{,}00{,}000
$$

### Step 6: CPI

$$
\text{CPI} = \frac{\text{EV}}{\text{AC}} = \frac{8{,}00{,}000}{10{,}00{,}000} = 0.80
$$

### Step 7: SPI

$$
\text{SPI} = \frac{\text{EV}}{\text{PV}} = \frac{8{,}00{,}000}{9{,}00{,}000} = 0.889
$$

### Step 8: EAC

$$
\text{EAC} = \frac{\text{BAC}}{\text{CPI}} = \frac{20{,}00{,}000}{0.80} = ₹25{,}00{,}000
$$

### Step 9: VAC

$$
\text{VAC} = \text{BAC} - \text{EAC} = 20{,}00{,}000 - 25{,}00{,}000 = -₹5{,}00{,}000
$$

### Step 10: TCPI (assuming BAC must be met)

$$
\text{TCPI} = \frac{20{,}00{,}000 - 8{,}00{,}000}{20{,}00{,}000 - 10{,}00{,}000} = \frac{12{,}00{,}000}{10{,}00{,}000} = 1.20
$$

### Step 11: Schedule Slip (assuming even monthly PV distribution)

Daily PV rate = ₹9,00,000 / (5 × 30) = ₹6,000/day

$$
\text{Slip Days} = \frac{\vert \text{SV} \vert}{\text{Daily PV}} = \frac{1{,}00{,}000}{6{,}000} \approx 16.67 \text{ days behind}
$$

**Analytical Matrix Summary:**

| Metric | Value | Status | Decision Trigger |
|--------|-------|--------|------------------|
| CV | −₹2,00,000 | ⚠️ Over Budget | Cost control action |
| SV | −₹1,00,000 | ⚠️ Behind Schedule | Schedule recovery |
| CPI | 0.80 | 🔴 Unfavorable | Investigate cost overruns |
| SPI | 0.889 | 🟡 Marginal | Accelerate critical path |
| EAC | ₹25,00,000 | ⚠️ ₹5L overrun forecast | Budget revision needed |
| TCPI | 1.20 | 🟡 Stretch target | Performance improvement |

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Control Chart Construction

> **Quality Data:** A software project tracks **defects per 1000 lines of code (KLOC)** across 8 sprints:

| Sprint | Defects (c) |
|--------|-------------|
| 1 | 12 |
| 2 | 15 |
| 3 | 10 |
| 4 | 14 |
| 5 | 13 |
| 6 | 16 |
| 7 | 11 |
| 8 | 9 |

### Step 1: Calculate Centerline (Mean)

$$
\bar{c} = \frac{\sum c_i}{n} = \frac{12+15+10+14+13+16+11+9}{8} = \frac{100}{8} = 12.5
$$

### Step 2: Compute Standard Deviation

$$
\sigma_c = \sqrt{\bar{c}} = \sqrt{12.5} \approx 3.536
$$

### Step 3: Compute UCL and LCL

$$
\text{UCL} = \bar{c} + 3\sigma_c = 12.5 + 3(3.536) = 12.5 + 10.608 \approx 23.11
$$

$$
\text{LCL} = \bar{c} - 3\sigma_c = 12.5 - 10.608 \approx 1.89
$$

### Step 4: Plot & Interpret

Since all observed defect counts (9–16) lie **within** [1.89, 23.11], the process is **in statistical control**. If sprint 9 reported **25 defects**, it would breach UCL → **investigation required** (assignable cause exists).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Project Control System Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A0[Project Authorization] --> A1[Establish Performance Baseline]
    A1 --> A2[Scope Baseline]
    A1 --> A3[Schedule Baseline]
    A1 --> A4[Cost Baseline]
    A2 --> B1[Execute Project Work]
    A3 --> B1
    A4 --> B1
    B1 --> C1[Work Performance Data]
    C1 --> C2[Cost Data Ledger]
    C1 --> C3[Schedule Tracker]
    C1 --> C4[Quality Inspection]
    C2 --> D1[Measurement Layer]
    C3 --> D1
    C4 --> D1
    D1 --> D2[Compute PV EV AC]
    D2 --> D3[Variance Analysis]
    D3 --> D4[Index Computation CPI SPI]
    D4 --> E1{Threshold Check}
    E1 -->|Within Limits| F1[Continue Monitoring]
    E1 -->|Breach Detected| F2[Trigger Corrective Action]
    F2 --> G1[Change Control Board Review]
    G1 --> G2[Approve Reject Defer]
    G2 -->|Approved| G3[Implement Action]
    G3 --> G4[Update PM Plan and Baseline]
    G4 --> B1
    G2 -->|Rejected| F1
    F1 --> C1
    D4 --> H1[Status Reports and Dashboards]
    H1 --> H2[Stakeholder Communication]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Earned Value Management Information Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Project Work Plan] --> P2[Cost Baseline]
    P2 --> P3[Planned Value PV]
    E1[Executed Work] --> E2[Work Completion %]
    E2 --> E3[Earned Value EV]
    C1[Incurred Costs] --> C2[Actual Cost AC]
    P3 --> V1[Variance Computation Block]
    E3 --> V1
    C2 --> V1
    V1 --> V2[Cost Variance CV]
    V1 --> V3[Schedule Variance SV]
    V2 --> F1[Forecasting Engine]
    V3 --> F1
    F1 --> F2[Estimate at Completion EAC]
    F1 --> F3[To Complete Performance Index TCPI]
    F1 --> F4[Variance at Completion VAC]
    F2 --> R1[Management Reports]
    F3 --> R1
    F4 --> R1
    R1 --> R2[Project Manager Decision]
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram: Statistical Control Chart Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Collect Quality Samples] --> S2[Compute Sample Statistic Mean Range Proportion]
    S2 --> S3[Calculate Center Line CL]
    S2 --> S4[Calculate UCL using 3 Sigma Rule]
    S2 --> S5[Calculate LCL using 3 Sigma Rule]
    S3 --> P1[Plot Control Chart]
    S4 --> P1
    S5 --> P1
    P1 --> P2{Sample Points Within Limits}
    P2 -->|Yes| P3[Process In Control]
    P2 -->|No| P4[Out of Control Signal]
    P4 --> P5[Identify Assignable Cause]
    P5 --> P6[Implement Corrective Action]
    P6 --> P7[Recalculate Control Limits]
    P7 --> P1
    P3 --> P8[Continue Monitoring]
```

## 4.4 Block Diagram: Integrated Change Control Loop

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    I1[Performance Variance Detected] --> I2[Generate Change Request]
    I2 --> I3[Impact Analysis Scope Schedule Cost Risk]
    I3 --> I4[Change Control Board CCB Review]
    I4 --> I5{Decision}
    I5 -->|Approved| I6[Update Baselines]
    I5 -->|Rejected| I7[Document Rationale]
    I5 -->|Deferred| I8[Add to Future Backlog]
    I6 --> I9[Re-baseline and Communicate]
    I9 --> I10[Implement Modified Plan]
    I10 --> I11[New Performance Monitoring Cycle]
    I11 --> I1
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation Note:** All four diagrams together constitute the **Complete Control System Blueprint**. The first shows the overall loop, the second zooms into EVM, the third into SPC, and the fourth into Change Control.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Define Earned Value Management (EVM). List any FOUR key performance metrics used in EVM.**

**Model Answer:**

**EVM Definition:** Earned Value Management is a project performance measurement technique that integrates scope, schedule, and cost to assess project progress objectively. It compares three dimensions — **Planned Value (PV)**, **Earned Value (EV)**, and **Actual Cost (AC)** — to determine project health.

**Four Key Metrics:**

1. **Cost Variance (CV) = EV − AC** — Absolute cost deviation.
2. **Schedule Variance (SV) = EV − PV** — Absolute schedule deviation.
3. **Cost Performance Index (CPI) = EV / AC** — Cost efficiency ratio.
4. **Schedule Performance Index (SPI) = EV / PV** — Schedule efficiency ratio.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Valuation Tip (3 marks):** Award **1 mark** for definition, **0.5 marks each** for the four metrics — keep formulas crisp, no derivations needed at Part A level.

---

### Question A2: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Differentiate between Control Limits and Specification Limits with an example from project quality management.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Control Limits | Specification Limits |
|--------|----------------|----------------------|
| **Definition** | Statistical boundaries of inherent process variation | Customer/contractual acceptable boundaries |
| **Set By** | Calculated from process data (μ ± 3σ) | Defined by client/design requirements |
| **Purpose** | Detect process instability | Determine product acceptability |
| **Example (Concrete)** | Concrete compressive strength: UCL = 35 MPa, LCL = 25 MPa | Specification: ≥ 30 MPa required by client |
| **Breach Action** | Investigate assignable cause | Reject product / rework |

> [!NOTE]
> **Valuation Tip:** Award **1.5 marks** for correct distinction and **1.5 marks** for a relevant engineering example (e.g., concrete strength, software defect density).

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Modified]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **design architecture** of a Project Control System. Discuss the **five-layer architecture** with the function of each layer.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A software project has the following data: BAC = ₹50,00,000; Duration = 12 months. At the end of month 6: **% complete = 40%**, **% scheduled = 50%**, **Actual Cost = ₹25,00,000**.

Calculate:
(i) PV, EV, AC
(ii) CV, SV
(iii) CPI, SPI
(iv) EAC, VAC, TCPI
(v) Interpret the project status and recommend control actions.

---

#### Model Solution:

### Part (a) — Five-Layer Control System Architecture [7 marks]

The control system is designed in **five interconnected layers**, each performing a distinct function:

**Layer 1 — Baseline Layer (1 mark):**
Establishes the reference standard for comparison. Includes the Scope Baseline (WBS), Schedule Baseline (Gantt Chart), and Cost Baseline (Time-Phased Budget / S-Curve). The baseline is **frozen at project authorization** and serves as the benchmark.

**Layer 2 — Measurement Layer (1.5 marks):**
Acts as the "sensors" of the control system. Collects work performance data through timesheets, cost ledgers, quality inspection reports, and progress photographs. This data feeds the Earned Value calculations and quality metrics.

**Layer 3 — Analysis Layer (1.5 marks):**
The "brain" of the system. Computes variances (CV, SV), performance indices (CPI, SPI), and forecasts (EAC, ETC, TCPI). Applies statistical process control (control charts) for quality dimensions. Identifies **trends, patterns, and anomalies**.

**Layer 4 — Decision Layer (1.5 marks):**
Compares computed metrics against **pre-defined thresholds** (e.g., |CPI − 1| > 0.1 triggers investigation). Evaluates corrective action alternatives, performs impact analysis, and routes recommendations to the Change Control Board (CCB).

**Layer 5 — Action Layer (1.5 marks):**
Executes approved corrective/preventive actions. Updates the project management plan, re-baselines if necessary, and communicates decisions to stakeholders. Feeds back into the Measurement Layer, closing the **PDCA loop**.

> [!NOTE]
> The five layers operate cyclically — outputs of Layer 5 become inputs to Layer 1, creating a **continuous improvement cycle** aligned with the Deming PDCA philosophy.

### Part (b) — EVM Numerical Solution [7 marks]

#### Step 1: Compute PV, EV, AC [1 mark]

$$
\text{PV} = 0.50 \times 50{,}00{,}000 = ₹25{,}00{,}000
$$

$$
\text{EV} = 0.40 \times 50{,}00{,}000 = ₹20{,}00{,}000
$$

$$
\text{AC} = ₹25{,}00{,}000
$$

#### Step 2: Variance Computation [1 mark]

$$
\text{CV} = \text{EV} - \text{AC} = 20{,}00{,}000 - 25{,}00{,}000 = -₹5{,}00{,}000
$$

$$
\text{SV} = \text{EV} - \text{PV} = 20{,}00{,}000 - 25{,}00{,}000 = -₹5{,}00{,}000
$$

#### Step 3: Performance Indices [1 mark]

$$
\text{CPI} = \frac{20{,}00{,}000}{25{,}00{,}000} = 0.80
$$

$$
\text{SPI} = \frac{20{,}00{,}000}{25{,}00{,}000} = 0.80
$$

#### Step 4: Forecasts [2 marks]

$$
\text{EAC} = \frac{\text{BAC}}{\text{CPI}} = \frac{50{,}00{,}000}{0.80} = ₹62{,}50{,}000
$$

$$
\text{VAC} = \text{BAC} - \text{EAC} = 50{,}00{,}000 - 62{,}50{,}000 = -₹12{,}50{,}000
$$

$$
\text{TCPI} = \frac{\text{BAC} - \text{EV}}{\text{BAC} - \text{AC}} = \frac{50{,}00{,}000 - 20{,}00{,}000}{50{,}00{,}000 - 25{,}00{,}000} = \frac{30{,}00{,}000}{25{,}00{,}000} = 1.20
$$

#### Step 5: Interpretation & Recommendations [2 marks]

**Status:**

- **CPI = 0.80** → For every ₹1 spent, only ₹0.80 of work is earned (🔴 Over budget by 20%).
- **SPI = 0.80** → Only 80% of scheduled work is completed (🔴 Behind schedule by 20%).
- **EAC = ₹62.5L** vs **BAC = ₹50L** → Projected overrun of **₹12.5L (25%)**.
- **TCPI = 1.20** → Requires 20% efficiency improvement to recover.

**Recommended Control Actions:**

1. **Cost Recovery:** Conduct root-cause analysis on cost overruns; negotiate vendor rates; reduce non-critical scope.
2. **Schedule Recovery:** Apply **crashing** (add resources) or **fast-tracking** (parallel tasks) on critical path.
3. **Change Control:** Submit Change Request to CCB for **baseline revision** if recovery is infeasible.
4. **Risk Re-assessment:** Update risk register; allocate contingency reserves.

---

### Question B (Alternative): `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Modified]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain **Statistical Process Control (SPC)** in project quality management. With a suitable diagram, describe the construction of a **p-chart** for monitoring software defect proportions. Derive the UCL and LCL formulas.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A construction firm tracks the proportion of defective bricks in daily samples of **n = 200 bricks**. Over **10 days**, the average defect rate observed is **p̄ = 0.04**. Compute the **UCL and LCL** for the p-chart and interpret the process control status if day 11 sample shows **18 defective bricks**.

---

#### Model Solution Outline:

### Part (a) — SPC and p-Chart [7 marks]

1. **SPC Definition** (1.5 marks): Statistical monitoring technique using control charts to distinguish **common cause** (inherent) variation from **special cause** (assignable) variation.

2. **p-Chart Purpose** (1 mark): Monitors proportion of defective items in samples of varying size; ideal for **attribute data** (pass/fail).

3. **Construction Steps** (2 marks):
   - Collect k samples, each of size n.
   - Compute sample proportion: $p_i = \frac{d_i}{n_i}$
   - Calculate grand mean: $\bar{p} = \frac{\sum d_i}{\sum n_i}$

4. **Formula Derivation** (2.5 marks):

$$
\text{Standard error of } p: \sigma_p = \sqrt{\frac{p(1-p)}{n}}
$$

$$
\text{UCL} = \bar{p} + 3\sqrt{\frac{\bar{p}(1-\bar{p})}{n}}
$$

$$
\text{LCL} = \bar{p} - 3\sqrt{\frac{\bar{p}(1-\bar{p})}{n}}
$$

The **±3σ** corresponds to **99.73% confidence** under the normal approximation.

### Part (b) — Numerical Computation [7 marks]

**Given:** n = 200, p̄ = 0.04, day 11 defects = 18.

**Step 1: Standard Error [2 marks]**

$$
\sigma_p = \sqrt{\frac{0.04 \times 0.96}{200}} = \sqrt{\frac{0.0384}{200}} = \sqrt{0.000192} \approx 0.01386
$$

**Step 2: UCL [1.5 marks]**

$$
\text{UCL} = 0.04 + 3(0.01386) = 0.04 + 0.04157 \approx 0.0816
$$

**Step 3: LCL [1.5 marks]**

$$
\text{LCL} = 0.04 - 3(0.01386) = 0.04 - 0.04157 \approx -0.0016 \rightarrow \text{set to } 0
$$

**Step 4: Day 11 Sample Proportion [1 mark]**

$$
p_{11} = \frac{18}{200} = 0.09
$$

**Step 5: Interpretation [1 mark]**

Since $p_{11} = 0.09 > \text{UCL} = 0.0816$, the process is **OUT OF CONTROL** on day 11. This indicates an **assignable cause** — possible causes include raw material quality issue, worker fatigue, or equipment malfunction. Immediate investigation and corrective action required.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in EVM/Control System Questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing PV with Budget:** Students often write PV = Total Budget. **PV is the budgeted cost of work *scheduled* to date**, not the total budget. (Loss: 1–2 marks)
>
> 2. **Sign Convention Errors:** CV/SV positive = favorable; negative = unfavorable. Many students reverse this. (Loss: 1 mark)
>
> 3. **CPI/SPI without Units:** Always write "CPI = 0.8" with **explicit interpretation** ("for every ₹1 spent, ₹0.80 earned"). Just numbers get partial credit. (Loss: 1 mark)
>
> 4. **EAC Formula Mis-application:** Different EAC formulas exist for different scenarios. Always state the **assumption** before computing. (Loss: 2 marks)
>
> 5. **Control Chart LCL Negative:** Never leave a negative LCL; clamp to zero for proportion charts (defects cannot be negative). (Loss: 1 mark)
>
> 6. **Skipping Interpretation:** Numerical answers without status interpretation (over/under budget, in/out of control) lose up to 2 marks. KTU examiners explicitly reward "engineering judgment."
>
> 7. **No Unit Specification:** Always mention units (₹, %, days, defects). Unit-less answers are penalized.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ A **Project Control System** integrates scope, schedule, cost, quality, risk, and change controls under a unified architecture.
- ✅ **Baseline → Measure → Analyze → Decide → Act** is the canonical control loop (PDCA-aligned).
- ✅ **Earned Value Management (EVM)** is the cornerstone technique — memorize **PV, EV, AC, CV, SV, CPI, SPI, EAC, VAC, TCPI**.
- ✅ **CPI = EV / AC** and **SPI = EV / PV** — both target **1.0**.
- ✅ **EAC = BAC / CPI** assumes past performance continues.
- ✅ **TCPI > 1.0** signals the need for performance improvement to meet BAC.
- ✅ **Control Charts** apply the **3-Sigma Rule**: UCL = μ + 3σ, LCL = μ − 3σ (CL = μ).
- ✅ **p-chart** for variable sample proportions; **c-chart** for constant sample defect counts; **X-bar / R-chart** for variable data.
- ✅ **Change Control Board (CCB)** reviews all baseline changes; without CCB approval, no re-baselining is valid.
- ✅ **S-Curve** plots cumulative PV, EV, AC over time — divergences indicate variance.
- ✅ Control system must be **proactive, not reactive** — define thresholds before project execution begins.
- ✅ Always interpret numbers: **numbers without interpretation earn only partial marks** in KTU valuations.
- ✅ Memorize the **threshold rule**: |CV%| > 10% or |SV%| > 10% triggers a formal corrective action review.
- ✅ For software/IT projects, adapt SPC to monitor **defect density (defects/KLOC)** and **velocity** (story points/sprint).
- ✅ For construction projects, EVM + S-Curve + Gantt = the trifecta of project control tools.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Control of Change and Scope
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT863 - PROJECT MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Control of Change and Scope

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Scope Control (Formal Definition)

**Scope Control** is the process of monitoring the status of the project and product scope and managing changes to the scope baseline. It is one of the six processes under the **Project Scope Management** Knowledge Area as defined in the **PMBOK Guide** (Project Management Body of Knowledge).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition**
> Scope Control involves influencing the factors that create scope changes, controlling the impact of those changes, and ensuring that changes are processed through the Integrated Change Control process. The key output is the **updated scope baseline** and **change requests**.

In simple terms, *Scope Control is the act of ensuring that the project delivers exactly what was originally agreed upon — no more, no less — while formally managing every deviation.*

## 1.2 Integrated Change Control (Formal Definition)

**Integrated Change Control** is the process of reviewing all change requests; approving changes and managing changes to deliverables, organizational process assets, project documents, and the project management plan; and communicating their disposition. It reviews the impact of every change on the entire project holistically.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Idea:** Change is inevitable in any project. The goal is NOT to *prevent* change but to *manage* it through a structured, transparent, and disciplined system.

## 1.3 The Change Control System (Formal Definition)

The **Change Control System (CCS)** is a collection of formal, documented procedures that define how project deliverables and documentation will be controlled, changed, and approved. It typically includes the documentation, tracking systems, and approval levels required for authorizing changes.

## 1.4 Configuration Management

**Configuration Management** is a subsystem of the overall project management plan. It tracks and controls the physical and functional characteristics of a product, service, or result. It ensures that the description of the project product is correct, complete, and current.

---

## 🌟 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> **Imagine you are driving a car from Trivandrum to Bangalore using a GPS app.**
>
> - The **original route** is your **Scope Baseline** (the planned path).
> - During the journey, the GPS detects a **traffic jam in Coimbatore** — this is a **change request** triggered by an external event.
> - The app **recalculates the route** (assessing impact on time, fuel, distance) — this is the **Integrated Change Control** process.
> - You press "Accept" — this is the **CCB (Change Control Board) approval**.
> - The app **reroutes you via Salem** — this is **Implementation of the change**.
> - The new estimated arrival time is **updated** in the app — this is the **updated project baseline**.
>
> If the GPS just silently changed your route, you would panic. But because it **communicates** the change and asks for your approval, you stay in control. **That is exactly what Scope & Change Control does in a project.**

---

## 1.5 Boundary Distinctions (High-Yield for Exams)

| Term | Primary Focus | Who Initiates? | Key Output |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Scope Control** | Monitoring scope performance | Project Manager | Work Performance Information |
| **Integrated Change Control** | Holistic impact analysis of any change | Any stakeholder | Approved/Rejected Change Requests |
| **Change Control System** | Tools & procedures to manage change | PMO | Change Log |
| **Configuration Management** | Controlling product specifications | Configuration Manager | Configuration Status Report |

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Trick:** "SICC-CC" — **S**cope, **I**ntegrated, **C**hange, **C**onfiguration, **C**ontrol. All five Cs govern this topic.

---

## 1.6 Key Terminology Snapshot

- **Scope Baseline:** The approved scope statement, WBS, and WBS dictionary — the reference for comparison.
- **Work Breakdown Structure (WBS):** A hierarchical decomposition of the total scope of work.
- **Work Performance Data:** Raw observations and measurements collected during project execution.
- **Work Performance Information:** Data analyzed in context and integrated across knowledge areas.
- **Change Control Board (CCB):** A formally constituted group responsible for reviewing, evaluating, approving, rejecting, or defering change requests.
- **Configuration Item:** Any product, service, or result that has been baselined and is under configuration control.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Five Pillars of Scope & Change Control

### 🔹 Pillar 1: Plan Scope Management
Defines how project scope will be defined, validated, and controlled. Output: **Scope Management Plan** + **Requirements Management Plan**.

### 🔹 Pillar 2: Collect Requirements
Determines and documents stakeholder needs. Output: **Requirements Documentation** + **Requirements Traceability Matrix**.

### 🔹 Pillar 3: Define Scope
Develops a detailed project scope statement. Output: **Project Scope Statement**.

### 🔹 Pillar 4: Create WBS
Decomposes deliverables into smaller, manageable components. Output: **Scope Baseline** (WBS + WBS Dictionary + Scope Statement).

### 🔹 Pillar 5: Control Scope
The focus topic. Monitors scope and manages changes.

---

## 2.2 Inputs, Tools & Techniques, and Outputs (ITTO) — Control Scope

| **Inputs** | **Tools & Techniques** | **Outputs** |
|---|---|---|
| Project Management Plan | Variance Analysis | Work Performance Information |
| Work Performance Data | Trend Analysis | Change Requests |
| Requirements Documentation | Earned Value Analysis | Project Management Plan Updates |
| Requirements Traceability Matrix | Change Control System | Project Document Updates |
| Organizational Process Assets | Configuration Management | — |

---

## 2.3 Integrated Change Control — Detailed Process Steps

1. **Identify the Change** — Any stakeholder identifies a need for change via a formal change request.
2. **Log the Change** — The change is entered into the **Change Log** for tracking.
3. **Impact Analysis** — The project manager (or team) analyzes impact on **scope, schedule, cost, quality, risk, and resources**.
4. **Submit to CCB** — The change request with the impact analysis is forwarded to the Change Control Board.
5. **CCB Review & Decision** — The CCB can **Approve, Reject, or Defer** the change.
6. **Update Baselines** — If approved, the relevant baselines (scope, schedule, cost) are updated.
7. **Communicate the Decision** — All stakeholders are informed.
8. **Implement & Verify** — The change is executed and its effects monitored.
9. **Document Lessons Learned** — Feedback is recorded for future projects.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why the order matters:** Skipping impact analysis is the #1 reason KTU students lose marks. The CCB **must not** approve any change without a documented cost-schedule-quality impact study.

---

## 2.4 Earned Value Analysis (EVA) — The Heart of Scope & Cost Control

Earned Value Analysis integrates **scope, schedule, and cost** to give a holistic view of project performance.

### Core Variables

- **PV (Planned Value):** The authorized budget assigned to scheduled work.
- **EV (Earned Value):** The measure of work performed expressed in terms of the budget authorized for that work.
- **AC (Actual Cost):** The actual cost incurred for the work performed.
- **BAC (Budget at Completion):** The total authorized budget for the project.
- **EAC (Estimate at Completion):** The expected total cost of completing all work.
- **VAC (Variance at Completion):** The difference between BAC and EAC.

### Key Variance & Performance Indices

| **Metric** | **Formula** | **Interpretation** |
|---|---|---|
| Cost Variance (CV) | $CV = EV - AC$ | Positive = Under Budget |
| Schedule Variance (SV) | $SV = EV - PV$ | Positive = Ahead of Schedule |
| Cost Performance Index (CPI) | $CPI = \dfrac{EV}{AC}$ | $> 1$ = Efficient Spending |
| Schedule Performance Index (SPI) | $SPI = \dfrac{EV}{PV}$ | $> 1$ = Ahead of Schedule |
| Estimate at Completion (EAC) | $EAC = \dfrac{BAC}{CPI}$ | Forecasted total cost |
| Variance at Completion (VAC) | $VAC = BAC - EAC$ | Remaining budget cushion |
| To-Complete Performance Index (TCPI) | $TCPI = \dfrac{BAC - EV}{BAC - AC}$ | Required efficiency to finish on budget |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Exam Pitfall:** Students frequently write $\text{SPI} = \text{PV}/\text{EV}$. The correct direction is $\text{SPI} = \text{EV}/\text{PV}$. Memorize: **E**verything **E**quals **E**V on top (CV, SV, CPI, SPI all have EV in numerator).

---

## 2.5 Configuration Management Sub-Processes

1. **Configuration Identification** — Selecting and documenting which items are under configuration control.
2. **Configuration Change Control** — Managing changes to configuration items.
3. **Configuration Status Accounting** — Recording and reporting the status of configuration items.
4. **Configuration Audits** — Verifying that the product conforms to its specifications.

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Construction Projects:** Managing design changes in a bridge construction (scope creep prevention).
- **Software Projects:** Tracking feature changes via Git/version control (configuration management).
- **Aerospace Projects:** Strict configuration control mandated by FAA/ISRO regulations.
- **Defense Projects:** Earned Value Management System (EVMS) is legally required by US DoD.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example — Earned Value Analysis for a KTU-Style Project

### 📋 Project Scenario
> A B.Tech final-year team is building an **IoT-based Air Quality Monitoring System**. The total approved budget is **₹ 4,00,000** (BAC). At the end of **Month 6**:
> - Planned work (PV) = ₹ 2,40,000
> - Actual cost incurred (AC) = ₹ 2,60,000
> - Work actually completed represents (EV) = ₹ 2,20,000

### 🎯 Objective
Compute all key Earned Value metrics and interpret the project health.

---

### 🔢 Step-by-Step Calculation

#### Step 1: Cost Variance (CV)

$$
\begin{aligned}
CV &= EV - AC \\
  &= 2{,}20{,}000 - 2{,}60{,}000 \\
  &= -40{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** Negative CV means the project is **over budget** by ₹ 40,000 at the current point.

#### Step 2: Schedule Variance (SV)

$$
\begin{aligned}
SV &= EV - PV \\
  &= 2{,}20{,}000 - 2{,}40{,}000 \\
  &= -20{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** Negative SV means the project is **behind schedule** (only ₹ 2.2 L worth of work done against ₹ 2.4 L planned).

#### Step 3: Cost Performance Index (CPI)

$$
\begin{aligned}
CPI &= \frac{EV}{AC} \\
    &= \frac{2{,}20{,}000}{2{,}60{,}000} \\
    &= 0.846
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** For every ₹ 1 spent, only ₹ 0.846 of work is being earned. **The project is inefficient.**

#### Step 4: Schedule Performance Index (SPI)

$$
\begin{aligned}
SPI &= \frac{EV}{PV} \\
    &= \frac{2{,}20{,}000}{2{,}40{,}000} \\
    &= 0.917
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** Only 91.7% of the planned work is completed. **Behind schedule.**

#### Step 5: Estimate at Completion (EAC)

$$
\begin{aligned}
EAC &= \frac{BAC}{CPI} \\
    &= \frac{4{,}00{,}000}{0.846} \\
    &= 4{,}72{,}813.24
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** If current inefficiency continues, the project will overshoot the budget by **₹ 72,813**.

#### Step 6: Variance at Completion (VAC)

$$
\begin{aligned}
VAC &= BAC - EAC \\
    &= 4{,}00{,}000 - 4{,}72{,}813.24 \\
    &= -72{,}813.24
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** A negative VAC confirms the expected budget overrun.

#### Step 7: To-Complete Performance Index (TCPI)

$$
\begin{aligned}
TCPI &= \frac{BAC - EV}{BAC - AC} \\
     &= \frac{4{,}00{,}000 - 2{,}20{,}000}{4{,}00{,}000 - 2{,}60{,}000} \\
     &= \frac{1{,}80{,}000}{1{,}40{,}000} \\
     &= 1.286
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** To finish the project within the original budget, the team must improve efficiency to **₹ 1.286 of work per ₹ 1 spent** for the remaining work. This is **unrealistically high**, signalling that a formal change request (budget revision) is necessary.

---

### 📊 Analytical Matrix — Project Health Indicators

| **Metric** | **Calculated Value** | **Threshold** | **Status** |
|---|---|---|---|
| CV | -₹ 40,000 | $> 0$ ideal | 🔴 Over Budget |
| SV | -₹ 20,000 | $> 0$ ideal | 🔴 Behind Schedule |
| CPI | 0.846 | $\geq 1.0$ ideal | 🔴 Inefficient |
| SPI | 0.917 | $\geq 1.0$ ideal | 🔴 Lagging |
| EAC | ₹ 4,72,813 | $\leq$ BAC ideal | 🔴 Overrun Expected |
| TCPI | 1.286 | $\leq 1.0$ feasible | 🔴 Unachievable without change |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** All metrics are red. A formal **change request** must be raised to either increase the budget, descope the project, or replan the schedule. This is precisely where the **Integrated Change Control** process activates.

---

## 3.2 Derivative Form — Forecasting Project Completion Time

Just as EAC forecasts cost, the **Estimated Time to Complete (ETC)** can be forecast as:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Original Duration} &= \text{Planned Project Duration (PD)} \\
\text{Schedule Forecast} &= \frac{PD}{SPI} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

For our example, if the original duration is **12 months**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Estimated Duration} &= \frac{12}{0.917} \\
                          &= 13.09 \text{ months}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** The project will likely take **~13.1 months** instead of 12, an **overrun of ~1.1 months**.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Integrated Change Control Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    id1["Change Request Initiated"] --> id2["Log into Change Register"]
    id2 --> id3["Impact Analysis Scope Cost Schedule Risk"]
    id3 --> id4{"Change Control Board Review"}
    id4 --> id5["Approve and Authorize"]
    id4 --> id6["Reject with Reason"]
    id4 --> id7["Defer for Reassessment"]
    id5 --> id8["Update Baselines Scope Schedule Cost"]
    id8 --> id9["Communicate Decision to Stakeholders"]
    id9 --> id10["Implement the Approved Change"]
    id10 --> id11["Verify Implementation and Quality"]
    id11 --> id12["Update Lessons Learned Register"]
    id12 --> id13["Close Change Request in Log"]
    id6 --> id13
    id7 --> id13
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Legend:** The three exit paths (Approve / Reject / Defer) from the CCB Review diamond represent the only three legal outcomes of a formal change evaluation. Any project that silently implements changes bypasses this control loop and is at high risk of scope creep.

---

## 4.2 Configuration Management Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    cfgA["Configuration Identification"] --> cfgD["Configuration Items Baseline"]
    cfgD --> cfgB["Configuration Change Control"]
    cfgB --> cfgE["Approved Modifications"]
    cfgE --> cfgC["Configuration Status Accounting"]
    cfgC --> cfgF["Status Reports to Stakeholders"]
    cfgF --> cfgG["Configuration Audit"]
    cfgG --> cfgH["Verify Compliance with Specs"]
    cfgH --> cfgD
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The four canonical functions of configuration management (Identification, Change Control, Status Accounting, Audit) form a continuous feedback loop. The **Audit** function feeds back into the baseline, ensuring that the project product remains compliant with its specification throughout the project life cycle.

---

## 4.3 Scope Control Information Flow (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    blk1["Project Management Plan"] --> blk2["Work Performance Data"]
    blk2 --> blk3["Variance Analysis and Earned Value Computation"]
    blk3 --> blk4{"Scope Variance Detected"}
    blk4 --> blk5["No Action Required"] --> blk8["Continue Monitoring"]
    blk4 --> blk6["Initiate Change Request"] --> blk7["Route through Integrated Change Control"]
    blk7 --> blk8
    blk8 --> blk2
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Block Diagram Insight:** This shows the **closed-loop nature** of scope control. The monitoring process is continuous — work performance data is collected, analyzed, and either passed (no variance) or routed to formal change control (variance detected). This is why scope control is often called the *"nerve center"* of project execution.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 📝 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**Define the Change Control System. List any two of its components.**

**Model Answer:**

A **Change Control System (CCS)** is a collection of formal, documented procedures that define how project deliverables and documentation will be controlled, changed, and approved. It forms the procedural backbone of Integrated Change Control.

Two key components are:
1. **Change Control Forms** — standardized templates for submitting change requests.
2. **Change Control Meetings** — scheduled CCB sessions to review pending changes.
3. *(Any other valid: Change Log, Tracking Systems, Approval Authority Levels)* — **2 marks for naming + 1 mark for definition.**

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Warning:** Writing only "a system to control changes" is a generic answer worth 1 mark. You **must** mention *formal, documented procedures* and the *approval mechanism* to score full marks.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Distinguish between Scope Control and Scope Verification.**

**Model Answer:**

| **Scope Verification** | **Scope Control** |
|---|---|
| Process of formalizing acceptance of completed deliverables | Process of monitoring scope and managing changes to scope baseline |
| Focuses on **acceptance** of work | Focuses on **change** of work |
| Performed at the **end of each phase/phase exit** | Performed **continuously** throughout the project |
| Output: Accepted deliverables | Output: Work Performance Information + Change Requests |
| Done **by the customer/sponsor** | Done **by the project manager** |

*(2 marks for the table differentiation + 1 mark for the primary actor distinction.)*

---

## 📚 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

### 🔵 Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]

> **a)** Explain the **Integrated Change Control** process in detail with a suitable diagram. State the roles and responsibilities of the **Change Control Board (CCB)**. **(7 marks)**
>
> **b)** A project has BAC = ₹ 20,00,000. At the end of Month 5, PV = ₹ 10,00,000, EV = ₹ 8,50,000, and AC = ₹ 9,75,000. Calculate CV, SV, CPI, SPI, EAC, and TCPI. Interpret the project health. **(7 marks)**

#### 📖 Model Solution — Part (a)

**Integrated Change Control (ICC)** is the process of reviewing all change requests, approving or rejecting them, and managing changes to the project baselines, deliverables, and documentation.

**Steps in the Integrated Change Control Process:**

1. **Receive Change Request** — A formal change request is raised with justification and submitted to the PM. *(1 mark)*

2. **Impact Analysis** — The project manager analyses the impact on the **Triple Constraint (Scope, Time, Cost)** plus **Quality, Risk, and Resources**. *(1 mark)*

3. **CCB Review** — The Change Control Board evaluates the change. The CCB may:
   - **Approve** the change (with or without modifications),
   - **Reject** the change (with documented justification),
   - **Defer** the change to gather more information. *(1 mark)*

4. **Baseline Update** — On approval, the relevant project documents (scope baseline, schedule baseline, cost baseline) are updated through a formal revision. *(1 mark)*

5. **Communication** — All stakeholders are informed of the change disposition.

6. **Implementation & Verification** — The change is executed, and its impact is monitored.

7. **Lessons Learned** — Insights are documented for organizational learning.

**Roles & Responsibilities of the CCB:** *(2 marks)*
- **Chairperson (typically Project Sponsor):** Sets agenda, breaks ties, ensures governance.
- **Project Manager:** Provides impact analysis and recommendations (non-voting).
- **Functional Managers / SMEs:** Provide technical feasibility input.
- **Configuration Manager:** Maintains change log and configuration records.
- **Quality Assurance:** Assesses impact on quality standards.
- **Customer Representative:** Validates alignment with customer requirements.

*(7th mark: Suitable diagram showing the flow from request → impact analysis → CCB review → approve/reject → baseline update → implementation.)*

---

#### 📖 Model Solution — Part (b)

**Given:** BAC = ₹ 20,00,000; PV = ₹ 10,00,000; EV = ₹ 8,50,000; AC = ₹ 9,75,000

##### Step 1: Cost Variance (CV)

$$
\begin{aligned}
CV &= EV - AC \\
  &= 8{,}50{,}000 - 9{,}75{,}000 \\
  &= -1{,}25{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

📛 **Over budget by ₹ 1,25,000.**

##### Step 2: Schedule Variance (SV)

$$
\begin{aligned}
SV &= EV - PV \\
  &= 8{,}50{,}000 - 10{,}00{,}000 \\
  &= -1{,}50{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

📛 **Behind schedule by ₹ 1,50,000 worth of work.**

##### Step 3: Cost Performance Index (CPI)

$$
\begin{aligned}
CPI &= \frac{EV}{AC} \\
    &= \frac{8{,}50{,}000}{9{,}75{,}000} \\
    &= 0.872
\end{aligned}
$$

📛 **For every ₹ 1 spent, only ₹ 0.872 of work is earned.**

##### Step 4: Schedule Performance Index (SPI)

$$
\begin{aligned}
SPI &= \frac{EV}{PV} \\
    &= \frac{8{,}50{,}000}{10{,}00{,}000} \\
    &= 0.85
\end{aligned}
$$

📛 **Only 85% of planned work has been completed.**

##### Step 5: Estimate at Completion (EAC)

$$
\begin{aligned}
EAC &= \frac{BAC}{CPI} \\
    &= \frac{20{,}00{,}000}{0.872} \\
    &= 22{,}93{,}578
\end{aligned}
$$

📛 **Forecasted total cost = ₹ 22.94 L (overrun of ₹ 2.94 L).**

##### Step 6: To-Complete Performance Index (TCPI)

$$
\begin{aligned}
TCPI &= \frac{BAC - EV}{BAC - AC} \\
     &= \frac{20{,}00{,}000 - 8{,}50{,}000}{20{,}00{,}000 - 9{,}75{,}000} \\
     &= \frac{11{,}50{,}000}{10{,}25{,}000} \\
     &= 1.122
\end{aligned}
$$

📛 **The team must achieve ₹ 1.12 of work per ₹ 1 spent to finish on budget — extremely difficult.**

**Final Interpretation:** The project is in **critical condition** — both schedule and cost are in negative variance. A formal **change request** to either revise the budget, descope the project, or extend the timeline is **mandatory**. **(*1 mark for the conclusion.*)**

---

### 🟢 Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam - July 2024]

> **a)** What is **Configuration Management**? Explain its four major sub-processes with a neat block diagram. **(7 marks)**
>
> **b)** A construction project has the following: Original Duration = 18 months, SPI = 0.80. Forecast the new estimated duration and the schedule slippage. If the BAC = ₹ 9,00,000 and CPI = 0.80, calculate the EAC and project cost overrun. **(7 marks)**

#### 📖 Model Solution — Part (a)

**Configuration Management (CM)** is a subsystem of the project management plan that ensures the project product is correctly described, controlled, and delivered. It tracks and controls the **functional and physical characteristics** of a deliverable. *(1 mark for definition)*

**Four Sub-Processes:**

1. **Configuration Identification** — Selecting, naming, and documenting which items (hardware, software, drawings, code modules) are under configuration control. Each item gets a unique identifier. *(1.5 marks)*

2. **Configuration Change Control** — Managing changes to configuration items. All changes go through impact analysis and CCB approval before being implemented. *(1.5 marks)*

3. **Configuration Status Accounting** — Recording, reporting, and tracking the status of each configuration item throughout the project life cycle. Outputs include Configuration Status Reports. *(1 mark)*

4. **Configuration Audit** — Periodic formal review to verify that the actual product matches its specifications and that documentation is complete and current. *(1 mark)*

**Block Diagram:** *(1 mark)* — Show the four sub-processes in a flow: Identification → Change Control → Status Accounting → Audit, with the Audit feeding back to Identification as a continuous loop.

---

#### 📖 Model Solution — Part (b)

**Given:** Original Duration (PD) = 18 months; SPI = 0.80; BAC = ₹ 9,00,000; CPI = 0.80

##### Part 1: Forecasted Project Duration

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Estimated Duration} &= \frac{PD}{SPI} \\
                          &= \frac{18}{0.80} \\
                          &= 22.5 \text{ months}
\end{aligned}
$$

##### Part 2: Schedule Slippage

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Slippage} &= 22.5 - 18 \\
                &= 4.5 \text{ months}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** The project will slip by **4.5 months (25% overrun)** if corrective action is not taken.

##### Part 3: Estimate at Completion (EAC)

$$
\begin{aligned}
EAC &= \frac{BAC}{CPI} \\
    &= \frac{9{,}00{,}000}{0.80} \\
    &= 11{,}25{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

##### Part 4: Cost Overrun

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Overrun} &= EAC - BAC \\
              &= 11{,}25{,}000 - 9{,}00{,}000 \\
              &= 2{,}25{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** The project is forecasted to overshoot the budget by **₹ 2,25,000 (25% overrun)** if current performance trends continue. Both time and cost overruns are proportional at 25%, indicating a **systemic execution problem** requiring a root-cause change request.

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark Deduction Zones in Scope & Change Control Questions:**
> 1. **Confusing SPI formula:** Writing $\text{SPI} = \text{PV}/\text{EV}$ instead of $\text{EV}/\text{PV}$ — **deduct 1 mark**.
> 2. **Skipping the formula substitution step:** Showing only the final numerical answer without intermediate steps — **deduct 1 mark**.
> 3. **Forgetting to interpret results:** Computing CV, SV, CPI, SPI without stating what they mean — **deduct 1 mark**.
> 4. **Generic definitions:** Writing "Change Control is the process of controlling changes" without *formal documentation / approval* — **deduct 1 mark**.
> 5. **No diagram in long answers:** When a question says "with a neat diagram", a missing diagram costs **2 marks** in KTU valuation.
> 6. **Mixing up scope verification and scope control:** These are different processes; don't substitute one for the other.

---

## ✅ Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> 📌 **Rapid Revision Checklist**

- 🎯 **Scope Control** = Monitoring scope + managing changes to scope baseline.
- 🎯 **Integrated Change Control** = Holistic review of all change requests, considering impact on ALL knowledge areas.
- 🎯 **Change Control System** = The set of *procedures, forms, and tracking tools* used to manage changes.
- 🎯 **CCB (Change Control Board)** = The formally constituted group that **approves, rejects, or defers** changes.
- 🎯 **Configuration Management** = Identification + Change Control + Status Accounting + Audit.
- 🎯 **Scope Verification** (often confused with Control) = **Formal acceptance** of completed deliverables by the customer.
- 🎯 **EVM Formulas** — Always put **EV on top** in CV, SV, CPI, SPI.
- 🎯 **CPI = EV/AC** and **SPI = EV/PV** — both indices = 1.0 means perfect execution; > 1.0 means good; < 1.0 means trouble.
- 🎯 **EAC = BAC/CPI** — forecast cost assuming current performance continues.
- 🎯 **TCPI > 1.0** is a red flag; it means the team must work harder than economically feasible to stay on budget.
- 🎯 **Trend Analysis** = comparing performance over multiple time periods (not just one snapshot) to forecast future behavior.
- 🎯 Every change must be **documented, assessed, approved, implemented, verified, and learned from** — skipping any step is a process violation.
- 🎯 Scope **creep** is the uncontrolled expansion of scope; the CCS is the antidote.
- 🎯 In KTU exams, always **end the answer with an interpretation** of numerical results — numbers without meaning earn partial marks only.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Project Auditing - Construction and Use of Audit Report
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT863 - PROJECT MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Project Auditing - Construction and Use of Audit Report

---

# 🔍 Project Auditing – Construction and Use of Audit Report

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition of Project Auditing

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Project Auditing** is a systematic, independent, and documented examination of a project's processes, performance, deliverables, financials, and compliance status against the approved project management plan, baselines, organizational standards, and applicable regulatory frameworks. It is conducted to verify conformance, identify deviations, uncover risks, and recommend corrective actions to stakeholders.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context (aligned with PMBOK® Guide and PRINCE2® perspectives), project auditing is treated as a **monitoring & controlling** activity that provides *assurance* to sponsors, top management, and external regulators about the *health* of an executing project.

### 1.2 Formal Definition of an Audit Report

> [!NOTE]
> An **Audit Report** is the formal, structured written document prepared by the project auditor (or audit team) at the conclusion of the audit engagement. It records the audit objectives, scope, methodology, evidence gathered, findings, conclusions, and recommendations. The report serves as the official communication channel between the auditor and the audited parties / stakeholders.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy – "The Project Health Check-up"

Imagine your **car** running on a long highway trip. You cannot wait for the engine to seize before checking the oil. Instead, you stop every 5,000 km at a service centre where a mechanic:

1. **Inspects** the engine, brakes, tyres (auditing project deliverables).
2. **Compares** current wear against manufacturer specifications (baselines).
3. **Issues a written report** listing everything inspected, what passed, what failed, and what to fix.
4. **Recommends** actions before the next long drive.

A **Project Audit** functions identically:
- The **car** = the project (scope, schedule, cost, quality, risk).
- The **mechanic** = the independent auditor.
- The **service checklist** = the audit checklist based on PMBOK® / standards.
- The **service report** = the **Audit Report**.
- The **driver** = the project manager who acts on findings.

### 1.4 Why Project Auditing Matters (KTU Emphasis)

The KTU 2024 scheme, through its Outcome-Based Education (OBE) framework, stresses **CO4 – Project Monitoring, Control & Closure**. Project auditing directly addresses this outcome by:

- Providing **early warning systems** for schedule and cost overruns.
- Ensuring **regulatory and contractual compliance**.
- Validating **earned value metrics** and performance indices.
- Documenting **lessons learned** for organizational maturity.
- Strengthening **stakeholder confidence** and governance.

> [!TIP]
> **Industry Standard Metric:** Most global firms (PMI, IPMA, ISO 21502) recommend at least **one formal audit per project phase gate**, with **major audits** at *initiation*, *mid-execution*, and *closure*. The frequency for *high-risk* projects may be **monthly**, while stable internal projects are audited **quarterly**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Project Audits

Project audits can be classified along multiple dimensions. KTU frequently tests the following typology:

1. **Financial Audit** – examines budget utilization, cost variance, and financial controls.
2. **Compliance Audit** – verifies adherence to organizational policies, contracts, and regulatory standards.
3. **Performance / Efficiency Audit** – assesses whether the project delivers value efficiently (EVM-based).
4. **Quality Audit** – reviews quality management plan, QA/QC processes, defect metrics.
5. **Risk Audit** – re-evaluates the risk register and the effectiveness of risk responses.
6. **Procurement Audit** – reviews vendor selection, contract administration, and deliverables.
7. **Closure / Post-Project Audit** – conducted after project closure to capture lessons learned.

### 2.2 The Audit Process – Step-by-Step Logic

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Five Logical Phases of a Project Audit (per ISO 19011 & PMBOK®):**

1. **Engagement Planning**
   - Define audit objectives, scope, criteria, and resources.
   - Select the audit team (independence is paramount).
   - Develop the **Audit Charter** and **Audit Plan**.

2. **Fieldwork / Evidence Collection**
   - Gather data through interviews, document reviews, observations, sampling.
   - Apply the **5W2H** technique (What, Why, Where, When, Who, How, How much).
   - Validate **Earned Value** indicators and progress reports.

3. **Analysis & Evaluation**
   - Compare actual performance vs. baseline.
   - Identify root causes using techniques such as **Fishbone (Ishikawa)** diagrams or **5-Whys**.
   - Categorize findings (Major, Minor, Observation).

4. **Reporting**
   - Draft the audit report.
   - Hold an **exit meeting** with stakeholders.
   - Obtain management responses.

5. **Follow-up / Closure**
   - Track implementation of corrective actions.
   - Verify closure of audit findings.
   - Archive the audit report for organizational learning.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Audit-Relevant EVM Metrics)

| # | Metric / Formula | Definition / Meaning | Typical Threshold |
|---|------------------|----------------------|-------------------|
| 1 | $CV = EV - AC$ | Cost Variance – negative means over budget | $CV < 0$ = Concern |
| 2 | $SV = EV - PV$ | Schedule Variance – negative means behind schedule | $SV < 0$ = Concern |
| 3 | $CPI = \dfrac{EV}{AC}$ | Cost Performance Index – > 1 is favourable | $CPI < 0.9$ = Red Flag |
| 4 | $SPI = \dfrac{EV}{PV}$ | Schedule Performance Index – > 1 is favourable | $SPI < 0.9$ = Red Flag |
| 5 | $EAC = \dfrac{BAC}{CPI}$ | Estimate at Completion using current CPI | — |
| 6 | $VAC = BAC - EAC$ | Variance at Completion – negative means budget overrun | $VAC < 0$ = Concern |
| 7 | $TCPI = \dfrac{BAC - EV}{BAC - AC}$ | To-Complete Performance Index – needed future efficiency | $TCPI > 1.2$ = Critical |
| 8 | $\% Complete = \dfrac{EV}{BAC} \times 100$ | Physical / Value-based completion percentage | — |

> [!NOTE]
> **Legend:** **EV** = Earned Value, **AC** = Actual Cost, **PV** = Planned Value, **BAC** = Budget at Completion. These **eight formulas** are the most frequently tested EVM derivations in KTU Module 4 questions.

### 2.4 Construction of an Audit Report – Structural Anatomy

A KTU-compliant audit report should contain the following **standard sections**:

| Section | Purpose | Length Guidance |
|---------|---------|-----------------|
| **Title Page** | Project name, audit ID, date, auditor | 1 page |
